male-68-lmca.chiefComplaint: My wife says my face is drooping and I can't move my right arm
male-68-lmca.variantDescription: Acute left MCA ischemic stroke within tPA window

missedConsequences.Acute Ischemic Stroke: Delay in tPA leads to larger infarct. 1.9 million neurons die per minute.
missedConsequences.Hemorrhagic Stroke: Giving tPA to hemorrhagic stroke causes catastrophic bleeding.
missedConsequences.Hypoglycemia: Untreated hypoglycemia mimics stroke and causes brain damage.

commonPitfalls: Not activating stroke alert immediately - delays door-to-needle time
Forgetting to check glucose STAT - hypoglycemia is a stroke mimic
Missing tPA window by spending too long on history before imaging
Lowering BP too aggressively - permissive hypertension maintains perfusion
Not recognizing contraindications to tPA

mcqs.question: Symptoms began 2 hours ago. What is the MOST time-critical next step?

options.explanation: Should not delay imaging. Rapid NIHSS and CT is priority.

options.text: Obtain CT head and activate stroke team immediately
options.explanation: TIME IS BRAIN. CT rules out hemorrhage for tPA consideration. Door-to-needle goal is <60 minutes.
Permissive hypertension up to 220/120 if not giving tPA. Only treat if planning tPA (target <185/110).
MRI takes too long. CT is faster and sufficient to rule out hemorrhage and identify early ischemic changes.

mcqs.question: CT shows no hemorrhage. BP is 175/95. What are the BP parameters for tPA?

options.explanation: Too restrictive. Target is <185/110 pre-tPA.

options.text: BP must be <185/110 before and <180/105 for 24h after tPA
options.explanation: Correct AHA/ASA parameters. Higher BP increases hemorrhagic transformation risk. Use IV labetalol or nicardipine to achieve.

options.text: BP control is not required for tPA
options.explanation: Uncontrolled HTN increases bleeding risk with tPA.
BP can be gently lowered to <185/110. Permissive hypertension only applies if NOT giving tPA.

mcqs.question: CTA shows left M1 MCA occlusion. NIHSS is 14, ASPECTS is 8. Patient is 2 hours from symptom onset. What is the best treatment approach?

options.text: tPA only - thrombectomy not indicated within tPA window
options.explanation: WRONG. For LVO, tPA + thrombectomy is BETTER than tPA alone. Thrombectomy should not be delayed for tPA completion.

options.text: Thrombectomy only - skip tPA since going to cath lab anyway
options.explanation: tPA should still be given while preparing for thrombectomy ('drip and ship' or 'drip and drive'). tPA may help with distal clot.

options.text: IV tPA immediately PLUS mechanical thrombectomy
options.explanation: CORRECT. For LVO within 4.5h window: Give tPA immediately, do NOT delay thrombectomy. NIHSS ≥6, ASPECTS ≥6, M1 occlusion = excellent thrombectomy candidate. Combined therapy is superior.

options.text: Neither - NIHSS 14 is too severe for intervention
options.explanation: NIHSS 14 is moderate-severe but NOT a contraindication. Very high NIHSS (>25) in 3-4.5h window is relative contraindication for tPA, but thrombectomy may still help.

mcqs.question: A different patient presents 8 hours after symptom onset with NIHSS 16 and confirmed M1 occlusion. CT perfusion shows small core and large penumbra (mismatch). Is thrombectomy indicated?

options.explanation: Extended windows exist! DEFUSE-3 (6-16h) and DAWN (6-24h) trials expanded thrombectomy eligibility using perfusion imaging to select patients.

options.text: Yes - DEFUSE-3/DAWN criteria allow thrombectomy up to 16-24h with favorable imaging
options.explanation: CORRECT. DEFUSE-3 (6-16h): target mismatch ratio ≥1.8. DAWN (6-24h): clinical-core mismatch (high NIHSS with small infarct core). This patient with large penumbra and small core qualifies.

options.text: Only if tPA was given earlier
options.explanation: tPA is NOT required before thrombectomy. In late windows, tPA is not given (outside 4.5h window).
Extended windows apply to anterior circulation (ICA, M1) as well. Basilar occlusion has even longer potential windows.

allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden right-sided weakness and facial droop with aphasia. Classic left MCA territory.

allDiagnoses.name: Atrial Fibrillation

allDiagnoses.rationale: Especially in elderly. CT will identify.

male-68-lmca.oldcartsNarrative: "My wife called 911. She said I was fine at breakfast around 8am, then about 2 hours ago she noticed my face looked funny and I was slurring words. I tried to pick up my coffee cup and couldn't grip it. She asked if I was okay and I couldn't get the words out right. Symptoms came on suddenly - one minute fine, next minute this happened. I have high blood pressure and diabetes - take lisinopril and metformin. Also aspirin. Quit smoking 10 years ago after 30 years. I was on a blood thinner for irregular heartbeat but they stopped it about 2 years ago."

historyQuestions.text: What time exactly did symptoms start?
historyQuestions.answer: About 10:15am - we had just finished breakfast. My wife looked at the clock.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL for tPA eligibility. Window is from LAST KNOWN WELL (LKW), not symptom discovery. tPA: 0-4.5h. Thrombectomy: 0-6h standard, 6-24h with advanced imaging. This patient is 2h from LKW - within all windows.

historyQuestions.text: Did symptoms come on suddenly or gradually?
historyQuestions.answer: Sudden - one minute fine, next minute my face was drooping.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sudden onset ('thunderclap') is hallmark of vascular events (stroke, SAH). Gradual onset suggests mass, infection, metabolic. This sudden onset with focal deficits = stroke until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Were you awake when this started? Any chance you woke up with symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I was awake - eating breakfast. My wife saw it happen.
historyQuestions.reasoning: WAKE-UP STROKE: If symptoms present on awakening, LKW = bedtime. MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch can identify 'tissue clock' - patients may still be tPA candidates. This patient has witnessed onset, simplifying timing.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Lisinopril for blood pressure, metformin for diabetes, and a baby aspirin every day. Used to be on a blood thinner for my heart rhythm but they stopped it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review identifies risk factors and affects tPA eligibility (anticoagulants)

historyQuestions.text: What medical problems do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and diabetes. Also had an irregular heartbeat - atrial fibrillation - they found a few years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: HTN, DM, and AFib are major stroke risk factors. AFib suggests cardioembolic etiology.

historyQuestions.text: Are you on any blood thinners now?
historyQuestions.answer: No, they stopped it about 2 years ago. Only aspirin now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Anticoagulant use affects tPA eligibility: INR >1.7 on warfarin, therapeutic DOAC = relative contraindication. This patient is only on aspirin - not a contraindication. Prior AF without anticoagulation = high stroke risk.

historyQuestions.text: Any recent surgeries or trauma?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries. No falls or head injuries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: tPA CONTRAINDICATIONS include: major surgery <14 days, head trauma <3 months, prior ICH ever. This patient has none.

historyQuestions.text: Any seizure activity or shaking?
historyQuestions.answer: No shaking. Just the weakness and speech problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: STROKE MIMIC: Todd's paralysis (post-ictal weakness) accounts for ~20% of stroke mimics. Absence of witnessed seizure makes this less likely, but non-convulsive seizures possible.

historyQuestions.text: Any headache?
historyQuestions.answer: No headache.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe sudden headache ('worst of life') suggests hemorrhage (ICH or SAH). Absence doesn't rule out hemorrhage - must get CT regardless.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had a stroke before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had a stroke or TIA.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior stroke within 3 months is tPA contraindication. Also relevant for DAPT: prior stroke + DM in 3-4.5h window is ECASS-III exclusion.

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before tPA and any other medications. No allergies allows full treatment options.

physicalExam.finding: BP 178/98, HR 88 irregular, RR 16, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 97% RA. Fingerstick glucose 142 mg/dL.
physicalExam.reasoning: Glucose rules out hypoglycemia. Irregular HR suggests AF as stroke etiology.

physicalExam.finding: Right lower facial droop. Forehead spares (wrinkles forehead symmetrically). Unable to show teeth on right. Flattened right nasolabial fold.
physicalExam.reasoning: UMN pattern (forehead spared) = central/stroke. LMN pattern (forehead involved) = Bell's palsy

physicalExam.finding: Right arm: 1/5 (trace movement). Right leg: 3/5 (against gravity). Left side: 5/5. Right arm drift immediate.
physicalExam.reasoning: Right hemiparesis with arm > leg = classic MCA stroke pattern

physicalExam.finding: Expressive aphasia - understands commands but speech is halting, telegraphic. Can say 'yes' and 'no' but struggles with sentences. Naming impaired.
physicalExam.reasoning: Broca's/expressive aphasia localizes to left frontal lobe (left MCA territory)

physicalExam.finding: Eyes deviated to the LEFT (toward the lesion). Can cross midline with effort. No nystagmus.
physicalExam.reasoning: Gaze preference toward lesion side (left hemisphere) is classic for large MCA stroke

physicalExam.finding: NIHSS = 14 (Level of consciousness 0, LOC questions 1, LOC commands 0, Gaze 1, Visual 0, Facial 2, Motor arm R 3, Motor arm L 0, Motor leg R 2, Motor leg L 0, Ataxia 0, Sensory 1, Language 2, Dysarthria 1, Neglect 1). Range 0-42.
physicalExam.reasoning: NIHSS 14 = moderate-severe stroke. Per AHA/ASA: NIHSS ≥6 suggests LVO and thrombectomy candidacy. NIHSS >25 = severe (higher hemorrhage risk with tPA). NIHSS ≤3 with high-risk TIA → consider DAPT for 21 days.

physicalExam.finding: Irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: AF is major stroke risk factor - likely cardioembolic etiology

physicalExam.finding: No carotid bruits bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bruit suggests carotid stenosis as possible source

labs.name: Point-of-care Glucose (STAT)
labs.reasoning: MUST check immediately - hypoglycemia is stroke mimic. This rules it out.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Platelets needed for tPA eligibility (>100,000). Rules out thrombocytopenia.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.reasoning: Baseline labs. Glucose confirms POC result.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: INR must be <1.7 for tPA. Normal here - patient not anticoagulated.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Rules out concurrent MI. Stroke can cause troponin elevation.

imaging.name: CT Head without contrast (STAT)
imaging.result: No acute hemorrhage. Subtle loss of gray-white differentiation in left insular cortex and left lentiform nucleus (early ischemic changes). ASPECTS = 8 (lost insular ribbon and lentiform). No mass lesion.
imaging.reasoning: CRITICAL - rules out hemorrhage for tPA eligibility. ASPECTS (Alberta Stroke Program Early CT Score) grades MCA territory: 10 = normal, deduct 1 point per region. ASPECTS ≥6 favors thrombectomy; <6 suggests poor candidate. Early ischemic changes within 4.5h do NOT contraindicate tPA.

imaging.name: CT Angiography Head/Neck
imaging.result: Left M1 MCA occlusion. Left ICA patent. Right circulation normal. No carotid stenosis.
imaging.reasoning: Identifies large vessel occlusion for potential thrombectomy

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Atrial fibrillation with RVR at 98 bpm. No ST changes.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms AF as likely stroke etiology

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No acute process. Heart mildly enlarged.
imaging.reasoning: Baseline, evaluate for aspiration if swallow impaired

treatments.name: IV Alteplase (tPA)
treatments.result: 0.9 mg/kg IV (10% bolus over 1 min, 90% infusion over 60 min). Max 90mg. This 80kg patient receives 72mg total. Door-to-needle goal: <60 minutes.
treatments.reasoning: TIME WINDOWS per AHA/ASA 2019: 0-3h = standard window (Class 1). 3-4.5h = ECASS-III criteria (Class 1, but exclude: age>80, NIHSS>25, anticoagulant use, DM+prior stroke). WAKE-UP STROKE: Can treat if DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI (tissue clock). Tenecteplase (TNK) 0.25mg/kg is emerging alternative (single bolus, may be superior for LVO).

treatments.name: Blood pressure management
treatments.result: Pre-tPA: Labetalol 10-20mg IV or nicardipine drip to achieve BP <185/110. During/after tPA: Maintain <180/105 for 24 hours. Check BP q15min x2h, then q30min x6h, then q1h x16h.
treatments.reasoning: AHA/ASA BP TARGETS: Pre-tPA <185/110, post-tPA <180/105 x24h (reduces hemorrhagic transformation). If NOT giving tPA: permissive HTN up to 220/120 (maintains collateral perfusion to penumbra). Only treat >220/120 if no tPA, or if end-organ damage.

treatments.name: Mechanical thrombectomy
treatments.result: Interventional neurology emergently consulted. Patient qualifies: LVO (M1), NIHSS 14, ASPECTS 8. Taken for thrombectomy.
treatments.reasoning: THROMBECTOMY TIME WINDOWS: 0-6h = HERMES criteria (LVO, NIHSS ≥6, ASPECTS ≥6). 6-16h = DEFUSE-3 criteria (target mismatch on perfusion imaging). 6-24h = DAWN criteria (clinical-core mismatch: large deficit, small infarct). This patient with NIHSS 14, ASPECTS 8, M1 occlusion at 2h is excellent candidate. Thrombectomy + tPA is BETTER than tPA alone for LVO.

treatments.name: Aspirin
treatments.result: HOLD for 24 hours post-tPA, then start aspirin 325mg
treatments.reasoning: No antiplatelet/anticoagulation for 24h after tPA due to bleed risk

treatments.name: High-intensity statin
treatments.result: Atorvastatin 80mg daily
treatments.reasoning: Secondary prevention - start during admission

treatments.name: DVT prophylaxis
treatments.result: SCDs applied. Hold pharmacologic prophylaxis 24h post-tPA.
treatments.reasoning: High DVT risk in immobile stroke patients

consults.result: Stroke team activated upon arrival
consults.reasoning: Stroke team essential for rapid tPA decision

consults.name: Interventional Neurology
consults.result: Consulted for thrombectomy evaluation
consults.reasoning: LVO identified on CTA warrants thrombectomy evaluation

consults.name: Speech/Language Pathology

consults.reasoning: Dysphagia common in stroke - aspiration risk

disposition.name: Admit to Neuro ICU/Stroke Unit
disposition.result: Admit for post-tPA monitoring and possible thrombectomy
disposition.reasoning: Post-tPA patients need ICU monitoring for hemorrhage, BP control, neuro checks q15min

disposition.reasoning: Post-tPA requires ICU-level monitoring

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Left MCA Ischemic Stroke (Cardioembolic - Atrial Fibrillation) - tPA + Thrombectomy Candidate
keyFindings: Sudden onset right hemiparesis (arm > leg) and expressive aphasia - LEFT MCA territory
Last known well: 2 hours ago (clearly within 4.5h tPA window AND 6h thrombectomy window)
Left gaze preference (eyes look toward the lesion)
UMN pattern facial weakness (forehead spared = central, not Bell's palsy)
NIHSS 14 (moderate-severe) - above threshold of 6 suggesting LVO
CT: No hemorrhage, ASPECTS 8 (≥6 favors thrombectomy)
CTA: Left M1 MCA occlusion = Large Vessel Occlusion (LVO) - thrombectomy indicated
ECG: Atrial fibrillation (cardioembolic source - will need anticoagulation for secondary prevention)
teachingPoints: TIME IS BRAIN: 1.9 million neurons die per minute. Door-to-needle goal <60 min. Door-to-groin (thrombectomy) goal <90 min.
tPA TIME WINDOWS: 0-3h standard (Class 1). 3-4.5h ECASS-III (exclude: age>80, NIHSS>25, anticoagulant, DM+prior stroke). Wake-up stroke: DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI.
THROMBECTOMY WINDOWS: 0-6h = HERMES (LVO, NIHSS≥6, ASPECTS≥6). 6-16h = DEFUSE-3 (perfusion mismatch). 6-24h = DAWN (clinical-core mismatch).
NIHSS SCORING: 0-42 scale. ≤3 = minor, 4-15 = moderate, 16-20 = moderate-severe, ≥21 = severe. NIHSS ≥6 suggests LVO → consider thrombectomy.
ASPECTS: CT scoring for MCA strokes. 10 = normal, deduct 1/region with early ischemic changes. ASPECTS ≥6 favors thrombectomy; <6 = poor candidate (large core).
BP MANAGEMENT: Pre-tPA <185/110. Post-tPA <180/105 x24h. If NO tPA: permissive HTN, only treat if >220/120 or end-organ damage.
STROKE MIMICS: 27% of tPA patients are DWI-negative! Common mimics: seizure/Todd's (20%), syncope (20%), conversion disorder (30%), migraine, hypoglycemia. ALWAYS check glucose!
DAPT FOR MINOR STROKE/TIA: If NIHSS ≤3 OR high-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4) → aspirin + clopidogrel x21 days = 32% RRR for recurrent stroke (CHANCE/POINT trials).
tPA CONTRAINDICATIONS: Recent surgery/trauma (<14d), GI/GU bleed (<21d), prior ICH, BP >185/110 uncontrolled, INR >1.7, platelets <100k, glucose <50, recent stroke (<3mo).
CARDIOEMBOLIC STROKE: AF is #1 cause. Start anticoagulation after ruling out hemorrhagic transformation (timing based on infarct size: small = 1-3d, moderate = 3-7d, large = 7-14d).
No antiplatelet or anticoagulation for 24h post-tPA due to hemorrhage risk. Then start aspirin, then transition to anticoagulation if AF.
FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911. Public awareness saves lives.

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: NINDS tPA Trial - Foundation of Stroke Thrombolysis
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: tPA within 3 hours increased good functional outcome (mRS 0-1) by 30%
evidenceBasedPearls.source: NINDS tPA Stroke Study Group, NEJM 1995
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: tPA is standard of care for ischemic stroke within 3 hours. NNT 8 for excellent outcome
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Favorable outcome at 3 months: 31-33% vs 20-21% placebo. Symptomatic ICH: 6.4% vs 0.6%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: tPA benefit extends to 3-4.5 hours with careful patient selection
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Exclude: age >80, NIHSS >25, oral anticoagulant, DM + prior stroke in 3-4.5h window
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: mRS 0-1 at 90 days: 52.4% vs 45.2% (NNT 14). sICH: 2.4% vs 0.2%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Mechanical thrombectomy + tPA superior to tPA alone for LVO strokes
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: For proximal LVO (ICA, M1), thrombectomy is standard of care within 6 hours
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: mRS 0-2 at 90 days: 32.6% vs 19.1%. Absolute benefit 13.5%. NNT 7

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Thrombectomy beneficial up to 24 hours in patients with clinical-core mismatch
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use DAWN criteria: high NIHSS with small core infarct (<31mL age≥80, <51mL age<80)
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: mRS 0-2: 49% vs 13% control. NNT 2.8 - one of the strongest treatment effects in medicine

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Thrombectomy beneficial 6-16 hours using perfusion imaging selection
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Use DEFUSE-3 criteria: ischemic core <70mL, mismatch ratio ≥1.8, penumbra ≥15mL
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: mRS 0-2: 45% vs 17% control. Trial stopped early for overwhelming efficacy

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Aspirin + clopidogrel for 21 days reduces recurrent stroke in minor stroke/high-risk TIA
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Wang Y et al., NEJM 2013 (CHANCE); Johnston SC et al., NEJM 2018 (POINT)
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Start DAPT within 24h of minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3) or TIA with ABCD2 ≥4. Continue x21 days
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: 90-day stroke risk: 8.2% vs 11.7% (NNT 29). Increased minor bleeding but not ICH

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: DWI-FLAIR mismatch can select wake-up stroke patients for tPA
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: If MRI shows DWI+ but FLAIR- (lesion <4.5h old), tPA is beneficial even with unknown onset

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Direct Thrombectomy vs Bridging (DIRECT-MT, DEVT, SKIP)
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Direct thrombectomy non-inferior to bridging tPA + thrombectomy in some populations
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: May skip tPA if going directly to thrombectomy, especially in Asian populations. Not yet standard in US.

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Tenecteplase as effective as alteplase with easier single-bolus dosing
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Tenecteplase 0.25mg/kg (max 25mg) single bolus is emerging as alternative to alteplase
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Reperfusion at 24h: 22% TNK vs 10% tPA in LVO patients. Simpler administration

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Intensive BP lowering (SBP <140) in ICH is safe but benefit uncertain
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Target SBP 140 in acute ICH within 6 hours if presenting SBP 150-220. Avoid SBP <120
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Modified Rankin ordinal shift favored intensive (OR 0.87) but primary outcome not significant

2018 AHA/ASA Guidelines: Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke - Thrombectomy

nihss.name: NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS)
nihss.description: Quantifies stroke severity and guides treatment decisions

thisPatient.clinicalAction: tPA eligible if within window. NIHSS ≥6 suggests LVO - get CTA for thrombectomy evaluation.

minor.action: Minor stroke - consider DAPT over tPA if non-disabling

moderate.action: Moderate stroke - tPA candidate, CTA for LVO

severe.action: Severe stroke - high mortality, still treat if eligible

nihss.lvoCorrelation: NIHSS ≥6 has ~80% sensitivity for LVO. Always get CTA.

aspects.name: ASPECTS (Alberta Stroke Program Early CT Score)
aspects.description: Quantifies early ischemic changes on CT to predict outcome and guide thrombectomy
aspects.scoring: 10 regions in MCA territory. Start at 10, subtract 1 for each region with early ischemic changes.

thisPatient.clinicalAction: ASPECTS ≥6 generally favors thrombectomy. <6 suggests large established infarct.

gcs.name: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
gcs.description: Assesses level of consciousness - used in stroke, trauma, and altered mental status

thisPatient.breakdown: E4 V3 M6 (eyes open, inappropriate words due to aphasia, obeys commands)

severe.interpretation: Severe brain injury - consider airway protection

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the CT showed hemorrhage?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but CT shows intracerebral hemorrhage

chiefComplaint.original: My right arm and leg won't work
chiefComplaint.modified: Same presentation - CT shows large left basal ganglia hemorrhage with intraventricular extension

shouldRise: Need for reversal if on anticoagulation

feedback.correct: ICH = NO tPA (absolute contraindication). Reverse anticoagulation if present. BP target <140 systolic (INTERACT2). Neurosurgery consult for possible evacuation. IVH may need EVD. ICU admission.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of hemorrhagic stroke. What's the BP target?
feedback.incorrect: Hemorrhagic stroke: tPA absolutely contraindicated. BP target <140 systolic (INTERACT2 trial). Reverse anticoagulation immediately (4-factor PCC for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet for Xa inhibitors). Neurosurgery for large/accessible hematomas.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Ischemic = tPA if eligible, permissive HTN if no tPA. Hemorrhagic = NO tPA ever, lower BP target (<140), consider reversal/surgery. CT differentiates and completely changes management.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient woke up with symptoms?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient woke up at 6 AM with deficits, last known well was 10 PM

chiefComplaint.original: My right arm and leg won't work
chiefComplaint.modified: I woke up and couldn't move my right side. I went to bed normal at 10 PM, woke up at 6 AM like this.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Wake-up stroke - may still be tPA/thrombectomy candidate

feedback.correct: Wake-up stroke: unknown onset BUT may still be candidate. MRI with DWI-FLAIR mismatch can identify <4.5h strokes (WAKE-UP trial). For LVO, DAWN (6-24h) and DEFUSE-3 (6-16h) criteria use perfusion imaging. Don't automatically exclude!
feedback.partial: Good recognition that wake-up strokes may be treatable. What imaging is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Wake-up strokes aren't automatically excluded from treatment. MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch: DWI+ but FLAIR- suggests stroke is <4.5h old. For LVO, extended window trials (DAWN, DEFUSE-3) use perfusion imaging to identify salvageable tissue up to 24h.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Known onset <4.5h = standard tPA criteria. Wake-up/unknown onset = MRI-guided selection may still allow treatment. Don't give up on patients with unknown onset.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if symptoms were mild (NIHSS 2)?
whatIfScenarios.description: Mild facial droop and arm drift only, NIHSS 2, symptoms improving

chiefComplaint.original: My right arm and leg won't work
chiefComplaint.modified: My face feels a little droopy and my arm feels weak, but it's getting better

shouldDrop: tPA (relative - can still give if disabling)

feedback.correct: Minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3): tPA is relative contraindication UNLESS symptoms are disabling. Still get CTA - 10-15% of minor strokes have LVO! Start DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel x21 days) for secondary prevention. Admit for workup.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of minor stroke. What's the secondary prevention?
feedback.incorrect: Minor stroke/TIA management: tPA generally avoided if non-disabling. BUT get CTA - LVO can present mild initially and worsen. DAPT x21 days (CHANCE/POINT trials) reduces recurrence by 32%. Full stroke workup: echo, Holter, vessel imaging.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Moderate-severe stroke = tPA + thrombectomy if LVO. Minor stroke = DAPT, still image vessels, admit for workup. Severity changes acute treatment but not need for full workup.

guidelineReferences.name: AAO-HNS BPPV Clinical Practice Guideline 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Bhattacharyya N, et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2017;156(3S):S1-S47
keyPoints: Diagnosis with Dix-Hallpike maneuver (posterior canal) or supine roll test (horizontal)
Epley maneuver is first-line treatment for posterior canal BPPV (Class I)
No role for vestibular suppressants for treatment
Avoid bed rest - reduces effectiveness of repositioning
HINTS exam to differentiate peripheral from central vertigo
Red flags: acute hearing loss, new headache, focal neurologic signs

female-55-bppv.name: Paul Rivera
female-55-bppv.chiefComplaint: The room is spinning when I move my head

missedConsequences.Posterior Circulation Stroke: Missed cerebellar stroke can cause brainstem compression and death.
missedConsequences.BPPV: Missed BPPV means patient suffers unnecessarily - easily treatable.

commonPitfalls: Not distinguishing central (dangerous) from peripheral (benign) vertigo
Missing cerebellar stroke presenting as 'just vertigo'
Not performing Dix-Hallpike test to confirm BPPV

allDiagnoses.name: BPPV
allDiagnoses.rationale: Brief episodes triggered by head position, positive Dix-Hallpike = classic BPPV.

allDiagnoses.name: Posterior Circulation Stroke
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cerebellar stroke can present as vertigo. Look for other neuro findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Vestibular Neuritis

allDiagnoses.name: Meniere's Disease

female-55-bppv.oldcartsNarrative: "For the past week, every time I roll over in bed or look up, the room spins. It only lasts 20-30 seconds but it's intense. I had this years ago and a doctor did some maneuver that fixed it."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: About a week ago. I woke up one morning and when I rolled over in bed, the room started spinning. It's been happening every day since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset of positional vertigo is typical for BPPV; very recent onset also requires ruling out stroke

historyQuestions.text: How long does each episode last?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe 20-30 seconds. If I stay still it stops.
historyQuestions.reasoning: BRIEF episodes (<1 min) triggered by position = classic BPPV.

historyQuestions.text: What triggers the spinning?
historyQuestions.answer: Rolling over in bed, especially to the right. Looking up. Bending over.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Position-dependent triggers are hallmark of BPPV.

historyQuestions.text: Any weakness, numbness, trouble speaking?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of other neuro symptoms argues against central cause.

historyQuestions.text: Any hearing loss or ringing in your ears?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my hearing is fine. No ringing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No hearing loss or tinnitus - argues against Meniere's disease

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea or vomiting with the episodes?
historyQuestions.answer: I feel queasy during the spinning but haven't vomited.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea common with BPPV; severe vomiting would be more concerning

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Lisinopril for blood pressure and a statin for cholesterol. That's it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications noted - not ototoxic; important before meclizine

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important if considering vestibular suppressants

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and high cholesterol. Had a knee replacement 2 years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cardiovascular risk factors noted but presentation classic for BPPV

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned this happened before?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, maybe 5 years ago. A doctor did a maneuver where she moved my head around and it fixed it right away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior BPPV with response to Epley supports diagnosis - recurrence is common

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_recent_triggers.answer: Had a cold 2 weeks ago. Also was at the dentist lying back for a while.
social_recent_triggers.relevance: Viral illness and supine positioning are known BPPV triggers.

recreation_head_trauma.answer: Bumped my head lightly last month.
recreation_head_trauma.relevance: Even minor head trauma can dislodge otoconia.

physicalExam.finding: RIGHT positive: upbeating, torsional nystagmus after 2-second latency, lasting 20 seconds. Fatiguable. LEFT negative.
physicalExam.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: latency, torsional upbeat nystagmus, brief duration, fatigue = posterior canal BPPV.

physicalExam.finding: Finger-to-nose accurate. No dysmetria. Gait steady when not vertiginous.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal cerebellar exam argues against central lesion.

labs.name: Labs not routinely indicated for classic BPPV
labs.result: For classic BPPV with positive Dix-Hallpike and characteristic nystagmus, no laboratory testing is needed.
labs.reasoning: BPPV is a clinical diagnosis. Labs do not confirm or exclude BPPV.

labs.name: CBC and BMP
labs.reasoning: Would only check if considering anemia, electrolyte abnormality, or renal disease as contributor. Not needed for typical BPPV.

labs.name: Fingerstick glucose
labs.reasoning: Quick check to rule out hypoglycemia if any concern, though BPPV has characteristic positional trigger.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Thyroid dysfunction can cause dizziness but not position-triggered vertigo with nystagmus. Not needed for classic presentation.

imaging.name: Imaging not indicated for classic BPPV
imaging.result: With positive Dix-Hallpike and characteristic peripheral nystagmus, no imaging is needed.
imaging.reasoning: BPPV is diagnosed clinically by Dix-Hallpike test. Normal neurological exam and characteristic nystagmus exclude central causes.

imaging.name: MRI brain
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED for classic BPPV presentation
imaging.reasoning: Reserve MRI for: atypical nystagmus (vertical, direction-changing), no latency, doesn't fatigue, cerebellar signs, new headache, focal deficits. This patient has classic peripheral findings.

imaging.name: CT head
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED for classic BPPV
imaging.reasoning: CT cannot diagnose BPPV and is insensitive for posterior fossa lesions. If central cause suspected, MRI is preferred.

procedures.name: Dix-Hallpike test (diagnostic)
procedures.result: Right positive with upbeating torsional nystagmus, 2-second latency, 20-second duration, fatigable. Left negative. DIAGNOSTIC for right posterior canal BPPV.
procedures.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC TEST of choice. Latency (2-5 sec), torsional upbeat nystagmus toward affected ear, brief duration (<1 min), and fatigability confirm peripheral cause (BPPV).

procedures.name: Epley maneuver (canalith repositioning)
procedures.result: Performed successfully. Patient experienced vertigo during maneuver that resolved. Instructions given for post-maneuver precautions.
procedures.reasoning: TREATMENT of choice for posterior canal BPPV. 70-90% effective with 1-3 treatments. Repositions otoconia from posterior canal back to utricle.

procedures.name: Semont maneuver (liberatory maneuver)
procedures.result: Alternative to Epley if Epley unsuccessful or not tolerated
procedures.reasoning: Alternative repositioning technique. May be preferred in some patients. Similar efficacy to Epley.

procedures.name: Supine roll test (if lateral canal suspected)
procedures.result: Not performed - posterior canal BPPV confirmed on Dix-Hallpike
procedures.reasoning: Tests for horizontal (lateral) canal BPPV. Perform if Dix-Hallpike negative but history suggests BPPV. Different treatment (BBQ roll or Gufoni maneuver).

treatments.name: Canalith repositioning (Epley maneuver)
treatments.result: Performed in office. Patient to sleep propped up tonight, avoid rapid head movements for 24-48 hours.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment. Highly effective. Post-treatment instructions: sleep semi-recumbent 1-2 nights, avoid lying flat, no rapid head movements for 24-48 hours.

treatments.name: Home Epley instructions
treatments.result: Taught home Epley technique for recurrence
treatments.reasoning: BPPV recurs in 30-50% within 1-3 years. Teaching home maneuvers allows self-treatment of recurrences.

treatments.name: Brandt-Daroff exercises
treatments.result: Instructed as home habituation exercise if needed
treatments.reasoning: Habituation exercises. Less effective than repositioning but can be done at home. Alternative if repositioning maneuvers unsuccessful.

treatments.name: Meclizine (vestibular suppressant)
treatments.result: Generally NOT recommended for BPPV. May prescribe short course (2-3 days) only for severe nausea.
treatments.reasoning: Vestibular suppressants slow central compensation and do NOT treat underlying BPPV. Brief use only for severe nausea. Contraindicated in elderly (anticholinergic, sedating, fall risk).

treatments.name: Ondansetron for nausea
treatments.result: 4mg ODT PRN for severe nausea
treatments.reasoning: Anti-emetic without vestibular suppression. Preferred over meclizine if anti-nausea needed.

consults.name: No specialty consultation needed for classic BPPV
consults.result: Primary care can diagnose and treat classic BPPV
consults.reasoning: BPPV is diagnosed and treated in primary care. No referral needed for typical cases.

consults.name: ENT / Otolaryngology
consults.result: Consider if refractory, recurrent, or atypical
consults.reasoning: Referral indications: fails multiple repositioning attempts, frequent recurrence, atypical features, suspected Meniere's or other vestibular disorder.

consults.name: Neurology
consults.result: Only if central cause suspected
consults.reasoning: Refer only if: atypical nystagmus, cerebellar signs, focal deficits, doesn't fit peripheral pattern. Not needed for classic BPPV.

consults.name: Vestibular physical therapy
consults.result: Consider for refractory cases or persistent imbalance
consults.reasoning: Vestibular PT helpful for: failed repositioning, persistent imbalance after successful treatment, other vestibular disorders.

disposition.name: Discharge home after successful repositioning
disposition.result: Epley performed successfully. Discharge with post-maneuver precautions and home Epley instructions. Return if symptoms persist >1 week or new symptoms develop.
disposition.reasoning: BPPV is outpatient diagnosis and treatment. Admit only if: cannot ambulate safely, intractable vomiting, concern for central cause.

actualDiagnosis.name: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV)
keyFindings: Brief episodes (<1 minute) triggered by head position
Latency, fatigability, and torsional upbeat nystagmus
No hearing loss or neurological deficits
Prior episode with response to repositioning maneuver
teachingPoints: BPPV = brief (<1 min) episodes triggered by position. Constant vertigo is NOT BPPV.
Dix-Hallpike: look for LATENCY, torsional nystagmus, BRIEF duration, FATIGUE
Epley maneuver is treatment of choice - ~80% cure rate

guidelineReferences.name: AAO-HNS Clinical Practice Guideline: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo
guidelineReferences.citation: Bhattacharyya N et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2017;156(3 Suppl):S1-S47
keyPoints: Dix-Hallpike test is diagnostic for posterior canal BPPV (strong recommendation)
Canalith repositioning procedure (Epley) is first-line treatment (strong recommendation)
Vestibular suppressant medications NOT recommended for routine use
Imaging NOT recommended unless atypical features suggest central pathology
Reassess within 1 month; repeat CRP if symptoms persist

guidelineReferences.name: AAN Practice Parameter: Vestibular Function Tests
keyPoints: Dix-Hallpike is gold standard for posterior canal BPPV
Lateral canal BPPV: use supine roll test
Central signs warrant MRI: direction-changing nystagmus, pure vertical nystagmus, no latency or fatigue
HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) helps differentiate peripheral vs central causes
Vestibular rehabilitation effective for chronic vestibular dysfunction

closureScript.diagnosis: You have BPPV - small crystals in your inner ear are floating in the wrong place, sending false signals when you move your head.
closureScript.plan: I just did the Epley maneuver to reposition those crystals. Sleep propped up tonight and avoid lying flat for 24 hours.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you develop weakness, severe headache, or constant vertigo, come back immediately.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This is very common and not dangerous. It often recurs but is very treatable.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were cerebellar signs?
whatIfScenarios.description: Vertigo with ataxia, dysarthria, and nystagmus that doesn't fatigue

chiefComplaint.original: The room spins when I roll over in bed
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm dizzy and can't walk straight. My speech is slurred.

feedback.correct: HINTS exam: if Head Impulse NORMAL (bad), Nystagmus direction-changing, or Test of Skew positive = CENTRAL CAUSE. Cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysarthria) = stroke until proven otherwise. Emergent MRI (CT misses posterior fossa strokes).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of central vertigo. What exam differentiates central from peripheral?
feedback.incorrect: Central vertigo red flags: unable to walk (peripheral patients can usually walk), direction-changing nystagmus, no latency/fatigue, cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysarthria, dysmetria). HINTS exam: central if normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew deviation. EMERGENT MRI (CT misses 50% of posterior strokes).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: BPPV = brief, positional, fatiguing nystagmus, can walk, normal neurologic exam. Central vertigo = constant, cerebellar signs, direction-changing nystagmus, can't walk. Central vertigo is STROKE until proven otherwise.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was hearing loss and tinnitus?
whatIfScenarios.description: Vertigo with unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and ear fullness

chiefComplaint.original: The room spins when I roll over in bed
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm very dizzy, I can't hear out of my left ear, and there's a loud ringing

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Meniere's disease or sudden sensorineural hearing loss

feedback.correct: Vertigo + hearing loss = NOT BPPV. Consider Meniere's (episodic vertigo hours long, fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, fullness) or SSNHL (urgent - steroids within 72 hours). Audiometry needed. ENT referral.
feedback.partial: Good recognition that hearing loss changes the diagnosis. What conditions cause vertigo + hearing loss?
feedback.incorrect: BPPV does NOT cause hearing loss. Vertigo + hearing loss: Meniere's disease (episodic vertigo lasting hours, fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness) or SSNHL (sudden hearing loss = ENT emergency, steroids within 72 hours). Get audiometry, refer ENT.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: BPPV = brief positional vertigo, NO hearing symptoms. Meniere's = hours-long vertigo, hearing loss, tinnitus, fullness. SSNHL = sudden hearing loss, urgent treatment needed. Hearing symptoms exclude BPPV.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the vertigo was constant, not positional?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe vertigo for 24 hours, worse with movement but present at rest too

chiefComplaint.original: The room spins when I roll over in bed
chiefComplaint.modified: The room has been spinning constantly for 24 hours. It's worse when I move but never stops.

feedback.correct: BPPV = brief (<1 min), purely positional. Constant vertigo = NOT BPPV. Vestibular neuritis: constant vertigo days, often post-viral, unidirectional nystagmus, positive head impulse test. Treatment: supportive, vestibular suppressants short-term, vestibular PT.
feedback.partial: Good recognition that constant vertigo isn't BPPV. What's the likely diagnosis?
feedback.incorrect: BPPV causes BRIEF episodes (<1 minute) triggered ONLY by position change. Constant vertigo = vestibular neuritis (viral, constant vertigo for days, unidirectional nystagmus) or MUST rule out central cause (stroke). Vestibular neuritis: supportive care, short-term meclizine, vestibular rehab. Do HINTS exam to rule out stroke.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: BPPV = seconds, purely positional, resolved between episodes. Vestibular neuritis = hours to days of constant vertigo. Central (stroke) = cerebellar signs, direction-changing nystagmus. Duration and constancy distinguish these.

guidelineReferences.name: ACP/APS Low Back Pain Guidelines 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Qaseem A, et al. Ann Intern Med. 2017;166:514-530
keyPoints: Most acute low back pain improves within 4-6 weeks
Imaging NOT recommended for acute LBP without red flags
Red flags: saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, progressive weakness
NSAIDs or muscle relaxants for acute pain
Physical therapy and activity modification over bed rest
Surgery reserved for refractory cases with concordant imaging

male-42-l5s1.name: Matthew Lopez
male-42-l5s1.chiefComplaint: Low back pain shooting down my leg

missedConsequences.Cauda Equina Syndrome: Missed CES causes permanent incontinence and paralysis. Surgical emergency.
missedConsequences.Herniated Disc: Missed diagnosis leads to prolonged suffering and potential chronic pain.

commonPitfalls: Not screening for cauda equina red flags (saddle anesthesia, incontinence, retention)
Over-imaging early when conservative treatment is appropriate
Missing motor weakness that indicates more severe compression

allDiagnoses.name: Lumbar Disc Herniation with Radiculopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic dermatomal distribution of pain, positive straight leg raise, sensory changes.

allDiagnoses.name: Cauda Equina Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia = surgical emergency.

allDiagnoses.name: Spinal Stenosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually older patients, bilateral symptoms, worse with extension.

allDiagnoses.name: Mechanical Low Back Pain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not have radicular symptoms or neuro findings.

male-42-l5s1.oldcartsNarrative: "Started 2 weeks ago when I was lifting boxes. Felt a pop in my lower back, then shooting pain down my right leg to my foot. The pain is worse when I sit or bend forward. I get numbness on the outside of my foot. Standing and walking actually help a bit."

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly does the pain go?
historyQuestions.answer: Starts in my lower back, goes through my buttock, down the back of my thigh, and into my calf and outer foot.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic S1 dermatomal distribution - posterior thigh, calf, lateral foot.

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with bladder or bowel control?
historyQuestions.answer: No, those are fine. No accidents or retention.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MUST ASK - absence of bladder/bowel dysfunction rules out CES emergency.

historyQuestions.text: Any numbness in your groin or buttock area?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just the leg and foot.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of saddle anesthesia reassuring against CES.

historyQuestions.text: Any weakness in your leg or foot?
historyQuestions.answer: My foot feels a bit weak. Hard to push off when walking.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Weakness indicates motor involvement - may need imaging.

historyQuestions.text: What were you doing when it started?
historyQuestions.answer: Lifting a heavy box at work. Felt a pop and sudden pain. Been bad ever since.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Mechanical trigger with lifting is classic for disc herniation

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing regular. I've been taking a lot of ibuprofen for this - probably too much.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High NSAID use noted - important for prescribing

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: Codeine makes me nauseous.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Opioid sensitivity - affects pain management options

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems or prior back issues?
historyQuestions.answer: Had some back pain before but never like this. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior back pain but no history of radiculopathy

historyQuestions.text: What makes it worse or better?
historyQuestions.answer: Sitting is the worst - especially driving. Standing up and walking around actually helps some. Bending forward hurts.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sitting worsens disc symptoms; standing relieves. Opposite of spinal stenosis.

historyQuestions.text: Is it just one leg or both?
historyQuestions.answer: Just the right leg. Left side is fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unilateral symptoms - bilateral would be more concerning for CES

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_lifting.answer: Warehouse work. Heavy lifting daily. No training on proper technique.
occupation_lifting.relevance: Occupational heavy lifting without proper mechanics is major disc herniation risk.

occupation_ergonomics.answer: Lots of bending and twisting. No back support provided.
occupation_ergonomics.relevance: Poor workplace ergonomics contributes to injury.

social_exercise.answer: No time for exercise. Always tired after work.
social_exercise.relevance: Weak core muscles fail to protect spine during lifting.

physicalExam.finding: Right SLR positive at 40° - reproduces radicular pain to foot. Left negative.
physicalExam.reasoning: Positive SLR is sensitive for disc herniation with nerve root compression.

physicalExam.finding: Right ankle plantarflexion 4/5 (mild weakness). Unable to heel raise on right. Otherwise 5/5.
physicalExam.reasoning: Plantarflexion weakness indicates S1 nerve root involvement.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased sensation lateral foot and posterior calf on right. Perianal sensation intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: S1 dermatomal distribution. Intact perianal rules out CES.

physicalExam.finding: Right ankle jerk diminished (1+). All others 2+.
physicalExam.reasoning: Decreased ankle reflex classic for S1 radiculopathy.

labs.name: Labs not routinely indicated
labs.result: No laboratory testing needed for uncomplicated lumbar radiculopathy
labs.reasoning: Lumbar radiculopathy is a clinical diagnosis. Labs do not confirm disc herniation.

labs.name: CBC, ESR, CRP
labs.reasoning: Would only check if concern for infection (discitis, epidural abscess) or malignancy. Red flags: fever, IVDU, immunocompromised, unrelenting pain, weight loss.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated initially
imaging.result: Imaging not needed in first 6 weeks for radiculopathy without red flags or progressive motor deficit
imaging.reasoning: GUIDELINES RECOMMEND: No imaging for acute radiculopathy without red flags. Most improve with conservative treatment. Imaging in first 6 weeks does not improve outcomes and leads to unnecessary intervention.

imaging.name: MRI Lumbar Spine (without contrast)
imaging.result: Large right L5-S1 posterolateral disc herniation with right S1 nerve root compression. Mild disc bulge at L4-L5. No canal stenosis. No infection or mass.
imaging.reasoning: MRI is gold standard IF imaging needed. Indications: symptoms >6 weeks, progressive motor weakness, cauda equina syndrome, red flags (cancer history, infection concern). This patient has mild motor weakness - reasonable to image.

imaging.name: Lumbar spine X-rays
imaging.result: Mild degenerative changes. No fracture, spondylolisthesis, or bony lesion.
imaging.reasoning: X-ray cannot visualize disc herniation or nerve compression. Only useful for: trauma (fracture), spondylolisthesis, or bony lesions. Not indicated for typical disc disease.

imaging.name: CT Lumbar Spine
imaging.result: Alternative if MRI contraindicated
imaging.reasoning: CT myelography is alternative if MRI contraindicated (pacemaker, severe claustrophobia). CT without myelography has limited soft tissue resolution.

procedures.name: EMG/Nerve Conduction Studies
procedures.result: Not typically needed acutely - consider if diagnosis uncertain after imaging
procedures.reasoning: EMG can confirm radiculopathy and localize level. Most useful: atypical presentation, multiple levels on MRI, peripheral neuropathy in differential. Takes 3 weeks for denervation changes to appear.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen)
treatments.result: Naproxen 500mg BID with food x 2-4 weeks. Already taking significant ibuprofen - counsel on appropriate dosing and GI protection.
treatments.reasoning: First-line anti-inflammatory. Schedule dosing (not PRN) for first 2 weeks. Add PPI if prolonged use or GI risk factors.

treatments.name: Muscle relaxant (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol)
treatments.result: Cyclobenzaprine 10mg at bedtime PRN for muscle spasm x 1-2 weeks
treatments.reasoning: Short-term use for associated muscle spasm. Sedating - use at night. Limit to 2-3 weeks to avoid dependence.

treatments.name: Gabapentin for neuropathic pain
treatments.result: Gabapentin 300mg TID, titrate up as needed for shooting/burning pain
treatments.reasoning: Useful for neuropathic component (shooting pain, burning, numbness). Start low and titrate. Side effects: sedation, dizziness.

treatments.name: Oral corticosteroid (prednisone taper)
treatments.result: Consider prednisone 60mg x 5 days taper for severe symptoms
treatments.reasoning: May provide short-term relief by reducing inflammation around nerve root. Evidence is mixed. Avoid in diabetics (glucose excursions).

treatments.name: Acetaminophen
treatments.result: Can add to NSAIDs for additional pain relief. Max 3g/day.
treatments.reasoning: Safe addition to NSAIDs. Less effective alone for radicular pain than anti-inflammatory agents.

treatments.name: Activity modification
treatments.result: Avoid heavy lifting, bending, twisting. Short-term activity modification with gradual return to activity.
treatments.reasoning: Brief activity modification appropriate. Prolonged bed rest NOT recommended (worsens outcomes). Return to normal activity as tolerated.

treatments.name: Ice and/or heat
treatments.result: Ice 20 min several times daily for acute inflammation. Heat for muscle relaxation.
treatments.reasoning: Ice for acute inflammation (first 48-72 hours). Heat for muscle spasm and stiffness. Patient preference acceptable.

treatments.name: Avoid or limit opioids
treatments.result: Patient has codeine sensitivity. If severe pain, tramadol short-term. Avoid chronic opioids for back pain.
treatments.reasoning: Opioids NOT recommended for chronic back pain per guidelines. Short-term use only if severe. This patient has codeine intolerance.

consults.name: Physical Therapy
consults.result: Referral for core strengthening, McKenzie exercises, body mechanics training
consults.reasoning: FIRST-LINE treatment. PT improves outcomes: core stabilization, directional preference exercises (McKenzie), ergonomic training. Start when acute pain controlled.

consults.name: Spine Surgery/Neurosurgery
consults.result: Not indicated initially - refer if failed 6-12 weeks conservative treatment or progressive neurological deficit
consults.reasoning: Surgery indications: cauda equina (emergency), progressive motor weakness, failed conservative treatment (6-12 weeks). Most disc herniations resolve without surgery.

consults.name: Pain Management / Interventional Spine
consults.result: Consider epidural steroid injection if not improving with conservative measures
consults.reasoning: Epidural steroid injection may provide temporary relief and allow participation in PT. Consider if not improving at 4-6 weeks.

consults.name: Occupational Medicine
consults.result: Consider for work restrictions and ergonomic evaluation
consults.reasoning: Work-related injury - may need work restrictions, ergonomic assessment, return-to-work planning.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with close follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 2-4 weeks to assess progress. Return immediately for red flag symptoms. PT referral placed.
disposition.reasoning: Vast majority managed outpatient. Clear return precautions for cauda equina symptoms essential.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: NOT indicated for uncomplicated radiculopathy
disposition.reasoning: Admit only for: cauda equina syndrome (surgical emergency), severe progressive motor weakness, intractable pain uncontrolled with oral medications, concern for spinal infection or malignancy.

actualDiagnosis.name: Lumbar Disc Herniation with S1 Radiculopathy
keyFindings: Radicular pain following S1 dermatomal pattern
Decreased ankle reflex on affected side
teachingPoints: ALWAYS screen for cauda equina: bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness
Most disc herniations improve with conservative treatment in 6-12 weeks
Imaging not needed initially unless red flags or progressive neurological deficit
L5-S1 (S1 root): posterior thigh, calf, lateral foot; decreased ankle reflex; plantarflexion weakness

guidelineReferences.name: ACP Clinical Practice Guideline: Noninvasive Treatments for Acute/Subacute Low Back Pain
guidelineReferences.citation: Qaseem A et al. Ann Intern Med. 2017;166(7):514-530
keyPoints: Most low back pain improves regardless of treatment
First-line: superficial heat, massage, acupuncture, or spinal manipulation
NSAIDs or skeletal muscle relaxants for patients needing pharmacotherapy
Do not use systemic steroids or opioids for acute low back pain
Imaging not indicated without red flags in first 6 weeks

guidelineReferences.name: NASS Evidence-Based Guidelines: Lumbar Disc Herniation with Radiculopathy
keyPoints: Natural history favorable - most improve with conservative treatment
Physical therapy with directional preference exercises recommended
Epidural steroid injections may provide short-term relief
Surgery indicated for: cauda equina, progressive motor deficit, intractable pain failing 6+ weeks conservative care
Discectomy effective for radiculopathy not responding to conservative care

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a herniated disc in your lower back that's pressing on a nerve going to your leg. That's causing the shooting pain, numbness, and weakness.
closureScript.plan: Most of these improve on their own over 6-12 weeks. I'm prescribing anti-inflammatories and muscle relaxants. Physical therapy will be helpful. Avoid heavy lifting and bending.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back IMMEDIATELY if you have trouble urinating, loss of bowel control, numbness in your groin/buttocks, or weakness gets significantly worse. Those are emergencies.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is painful and scary. The good news is most people improve without surgery. We'll get MRI if you're not better in 6 weeks or if symptoms worsen.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had urinary retention and saddle anesthesia?
whatIfScenarios.description: Disc herniation with cauda equina syndrome

chiefComplaint.original: Lower back pain shooting down my leg
chiefComplaint.modified: Back pain, can't feel my groin area, haven't been able to urinate all day despite feeling like I need to

feedback.correct: CAUDA EQUINA = SURGICAL EMERGENCY! Bladder retention (most sensitive), saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg symptoms, fecal incontinence. Emergent MRI, neurosurgery consult STAT. Decompression within 24-48 hours - delays cause permanent damage. Check post-void residual (>200mL abnormal).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of cauda equina. What's the time pressure?
feedback.incorrect: Cauda equina syndrome: massive disc compressing nerve roots. Signs: urinary retention (check PVR), saddle anesthesia, bilateral weakness, bowel incontinence. EMERGENCY MRI and neurosurgery within hours. Decompression within 24-48h critical - permanent bowel/bladder dysfunction if delayed.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Standard radiculopathy = conservative treatment, outpatient. Cauda equina = surgical emergency, immediate MRI, decompress within 24-48 hours. Bladder symptoms differentiate.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if foot drop developed over 48 hours?
whatIfScenarios.description: Rapidly progressive motor weakness - foot drop

chiefComplaint.original: Lower back pain shooting down my leg
chiefComplaint.modified: My foot has been getting weaker. Yesterday I could lift it a bit, now I can't lift it at all. I'm tripping.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Severe L5 radiculopathy with progressive motor deficit

feedback.correct: Progressive motor weakness = urgent MRI and surgical evaluation. Foot drop (L5) that's worsening may need decompression to prevent permanent weakness. Unlike stable mild weakness which can be observed, progressive weakness is an indication for earlier surgery. Don't wait 6-12 weeks.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of urgency. What's the surgical indication?
feedback.incorrect: Progressive motor deficit: stable mild weakness can be observed, but WORSENING weakness needs urgent evaluation. Foot drop that's deteriorating = MRI now, surgical consultation. Early decompression may preserve function. Different from cauda equina (no bladder/bowel) but still urgent, not emergent.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Stable mild weakness = can observe. Progressive weakness = urgent MRI and surgical evaluation. Trajectory of motor function guides urgency.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had a history of breast cancer?
whatIfScenarios.description: Back pain in patient with malignancy history

chiefComplaint.original: Lower back pain shooting down my leg
chiefComplaint.modified: Same back and leg pain - but patient had breast cancer 3 years ago, now treated

keyDiscriminators: Back pain = red flag in cancer patients
shouldDrop: Benign disc disease (until cancer ruled out)

feedback.correct: Cancer history = IMMEDIATE imaging. Breast cancer commonly metastasizes to spine. MRI whole spine (metastases can be anywhere). Even if exam looks like 'simple' radiculopathy, must rule out metastatic disease. Bone scan or PET may also be needed. Don't treat as benign disc disease.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of cancer as red flag. What imaging?
feedback.incorrect: Cancer history + back pain = red flag requiring imaging. Breast, lung, prostate, kidney, thyroid most commonly metastasize to spine. MRI whole spine (not just symptomatic level). Plain films miss 50% of metastases. Early detection critical - cord compression is devastating. Oncology and radiation oncology involvement.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Back pain without red flags = no imaging for 6 weeks. Cancer history = immediate MRI. Malignancy history completely changes the workup.

keyPoints: Carbidopa-levodopa most effective for motor symptoms
MAO-B inhibitors or dopamine agonists for early/mild disease
Dopamine agonists: higher risk of impulse control disorders
Deep brain stimulation for motor fluctuations refractory to medications
Address non-motor symptoms: depression, constipation, REM sleep disorder
Physical therapy and exercise improve function and quality of life

male-67-pd.name: Robert Jenkins
male-67-pd.chiefComplaint: My hand shakes and I'm moving slower

missedConsequences.Parkinson's Disease: Delayed diagnosis means delayed treatment that could improve quality of life. Falls risk increases without intervention.
missedConsequences.Drug-Induced Parkinsonism: Continued offending medication worsens symptoms unnecessarily.
missedConsequences.Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus: Missed NPH - one of few reversible causes of parkinsonism.
