Missing the bradykinesia and rigidity that distinguish PD from essential tremor
Not asking about medications that cause parkinsonism (metoclopramide, antipsychotics)
Forgetting to check for orthostatic hypotension - common in PD
Missing early cognitive symptoms that might suggest Lewy body dementia instead

mcqs.question: What type of tremor is most characteristic of Parkinson's disease?

options.text: Intention tremor - worse when reaching for objects
options.explanation: Intention tremor suggests cerebellar pathology, not PD.

options.text: Rest tremor - present when hand is relaxed, decreases with movement
options.explanation: PD classically causes rest tremor that diminishes with intentional movement. 'Pill-rolling' tremor is characteristic.

options.text: Postural tremor - present when holding arms outstretched
options.explanation: Postural tremor is more characteristic of essential tremor.

options.text: Physiologic tremor - fine tremor present in everyone
options.explanation: Physiologic tremor is normal and not pathological.

mcqs.question: Which finding, along with tremor, is required to diagnose parkinsonism?

options.explanation: Ataxia suggests cerebellar disease, not parkinsonism.
Parkinsonism requires bradykinesia (slowness of movement) PLUS at least one of: rest tremor, rigidity. Bradykinesia is the cardinal feature.
Reflexes are typically normal or mildly decreased in PD.
Sensory exam is normal in PD.

allDiagnoses.name: Parkinson's Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Asymmetric rest tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and shuffling gait in elderly male - classic PD presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Drug-Induced Parkinsonism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must review medications - antipsychotics, metoclopramide, and others can cause parkinsonism.

allDiagnoses.name: Lewy Body Dementia
allDiagnoses.rationale: If cognitive symptoms prominent or visual hallucinations present, consider DLB over PD.

allDiagnoses.name: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
allDiagnoses.rationale: Triad: gait disturbance, dementia, incontinence. Treatable with shunt.

allDiagnoses.name: Essential Tremor
allDiagnoses.rationale: ET causes action/postural tremor, not rest tremor. Usually bilateral, improves with alcohol.

allDiagnoses.name: Progressive Supranuclear Palsy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have early falls, vertical gaze palsy, axial rigidity more than limb.

allDiagnoses.name: Multiple System Atrophy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have prominent autonomic failure, cerebellar signs, or both. Poor levodopa response.

male-67-pd.oldcartsNarrative: "My right hand started shaking about a year ago. At first it was just a little tremor when I was sitting watching TV - my wife noticed it before I did. It's gotten worse and now my left hand is starting too. The shaking stops when I reach for something. But what really bothers me is I'm slowing down. Everything takes longer - getting dressed, eating. My handwriting has gotten tiny. I shuffle when I walk and almost fell a few times. I feel stiff all over. My wife says I don't smile as much anymore and my voice is quieter."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the tremor - when is it worst?
historyQuestions.answer: It's worst when I'm just sitting, like watching TV. When I pick up my coffee cup, it actually stops. Then starts again when I put my hand back down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: REST tremor that decreases with action is hallmark of PD. Essential tremor is opposite - worse with action.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned slowing down - can you give me examples?
historyQuestions.answer: Buttoning my shirt takes forever. I can't cut my food as well. Getting out of a chair is hard - I have to rock back and forth. Walking is slower.
historyQuestions.reasoning: BRADYKINESIA is cardinal feature of PD. Difficulty with fine motor tasks, slow initiation of movement.

historyQuestions.text: Did the symptoms start on one side?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, all right side first. Right hand tremor, right leg feels stiffer. Left side is catching up now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ASYMMETRIC onset is characteristic of idiopathic PD. Symmetric onset suggests other causes.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the falls.
historyQuestions.answer: Haven't actually fallen yet, but I've stumbled a few times. My feet feel like they stick to the floor sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gait freezing and postural instability occur in PD. Early falls suggest PSP instead.

historyQuestions.text: Any trouble with memory or thinking?
historyQuestions.answer: My thinking is fine. Maybe a little slower to find words sometimes, but my memory is good.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Preserved cognition early favors PD over DLB. Early dementia suggests DLB.

historyQuestions.text: Have you seen or heard things that weren't there?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Visual hallucinations early in disease strongly suggest DLB rather than PD.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Lisinopril for blood pressure, atorvastatin for cholesterol. That's it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No dopamine-blocking medications. Rules out drug-induced parkinsonism.

historyQuestions.text: Any constipation, lightheadedness, or urinary problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes - I've been constipated for years. And sometimes I feel dizzy when I stand up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Autonomic symptoms (constipation, orthostatic hypotension) common in PD and often precede motor symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: How is your sense of smell?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, I can't smell things as well as I used to. My wife complains I can't smell when food is burning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hyposmia (reduced smell) is early non-motor feature of PD, often preceding motor symptoms by years.

historyQuestions.text: How do you sleep?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife says I thrash around and act out my dreams. I've accidentally hit her a few times.
historyQuestions.reasoning: REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams) is strongly associated with synucleinopathies (PD, DLB).

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting any PD medications

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and high cholesterol, both controlled. Had my appendix out years ago. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No stroke history or other neurological conditions

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of tremor or neurological disease?
historyQuestions.answer: My uncle had shaky hands when he got older. Nobody was ever officially diagnosed with anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history in ~15% of PD cases; also relevant for essential tremor

historyQuestions.text: How has your mood been?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt down lately. Hard to enjoy things like I used to. My wife says I seem flat.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Depression and apathy are common non-motor symptoms of PD

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with bladder control?
historyQuestions.answer: I have to go more often, especially at night. No accidents though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Urinary urgency common in PD. Incontinence would raise concern for NPH

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your daily life?
historyQuestions.answer: I used to love woodworking but can't do the detailed work anymore. Getting dressed takes so long. I don't go out as much because I'm embarrassed by the shaking.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant functional and social impact - important for treatment urgency

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_past_exposures.answer: I was a welder for 30 years. Retired 5 years ago.
occupation_past_exposures.relevance: Welding involves manganese exposure which can cause parkinsonism, though this presentation is classic idiopathic PD.

environmental_pesticide_exposure.answer: We had a farm when I was young. I helped spray pesticides as a teenager.
environmental_pesticide_exposure.relevance: Pesticide exposure (paraquat, rotenone) is associated with increased PD risk. Important epidemiologic link.

social_well_water.answer: Grew up on well water on the farm.
social_well_water.relevance: Rural living and well water (potential pesticide contamination) associated with increased PD risk.

social_family_history.answer: My uncle had 'the shakes' when he got older. Nobody else.
social_family_history.relevance: Family history in ~15% of PD cases. May suggest genetic component.

social_caffeine_tobacco.answer: Never smoked. Drink coffee every day - always have.
social_caffeine_tobacco.relevance: Interestingly, smoking and caffeine are associated with REDUCED PD risk - protective epidemiologic association.

housing_fall_risks.answer: Old house with stairs. Rugs everywhere. My wife is worried.
housing_fall_risks.relevance: Fall risk assessment important - home modifications may be needed as disease progresses.

social_support_system.answer: My wife is healthy and supportive. Kids live nearby.
social_support_system.relevance: Good social support important for managing chronic progressive disease.

social_driving.answer: Still driving but I'm slower to react. Getting nervous about it.
social_driving.relevance: Driving safety assessment needed - bradykinesia and reaction time affect driving ability.

physicalExam.finding: BP 128/78 sitting, 108/68 standing (20 mmHg drop). HR 68 sitting, 72 standing. Orthostatic hypotension present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Orthostatic hypotension common in PD due to autonomic dysfunction.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly male with masked facies (reduced facial expression). Quiet, monotone voice. Stooped posture.
physicalExam.reasoning: HYPOMIMIA (masked facies), hypophonia (soft voice), stooped posture - classic PD features.

physicalExam.finding: 4-5 Hz rest tremor of right hand, 'pill-rolling' quality. Decreases with intentional movement. Tremor re-emerges when hands held outstretched (re-emergent tremor). Left hand has subtle rest tremor.
physicalExam.reasoning: REST TREMOR with pill-rolling quality is classic PD. Re-emergent tremor also characteristic.

physicalExam.finding: Cogwheel rigidity at right wrist and elbow, increased with contralateral activation (Froment's maneuver). Left side has mild rigidity.
physicalExam.reasoning: COGWHEEL RIGIDITY (ratchety resistance) is cardinal PD sign. Asymmetric, worse on tremor side.

physicalExam.finding: Finger tapping slow with decreasing amplitude (fatiguing). Rapid alternating movements slow and clumsy, worse on right. Difficulty arising from chair without using arms.
physicalExam.reasoning: BRADYKINESIA with decrement (movements get smaller/slower with repetition) is REQUIRED for PD diagnosis.

physicalExam.finding: Shuffling gait with reduced arm swing on right. Short steps. Difficulty initiating walking (start hesitation). Mild festination. Turns en bloc.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic parkinsonian gait: shuffling, reduced arm swing, festination, en bloc turns.

physicalExam.finding: Takes 3 steps backward to recover (abnormal is >2 or would fall without catching).
physicalExam.reasoning: Impaired postural reflexes - fall risk. Would be severely impaired early in PSP.

physicalExam.finding: Full range of eye movements. No vertical gaze palsy. Saccades mildly hypometric.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vertical gaze argues against PSP. Hypometric saccades can occur in PD.

physicalExam.finding: Alert, oriented. Follows commands. MMSE 28/30 (missed date and one recall item).
physicalExam.reasoning: Relatively preserved cognition supports PD over DLB. Mild deficits common in PD.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Hypothyroidism can cause bradykinesia and slowness mimicking PD. Always check in new-onset parkinsonism.

labs.name: Vitamin B12
labs.reasoning: B12 deficiency causes neurological symptoms including gait disturbance and cognitive changes. Rule out treatable cause.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 140, K 4.2, Cr 1.0, Glucose 98. All normal.
labs.reasoning: Rule out metabolic causes. Baseline for medication monitoring.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline. No evidence of infection or hematologic disorder.

labs.name: Ceruloplasmin (if age <50)
labs.result: Not indicated - patient is 72 years old.
labs.reasoning: Wilson's disease causes parkinsonism but presents before age 40-50. Would check in younger patients.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.reasoning: Baseline before starting medications that may affect liver.

imaging.name: MRI Brain without contrast
imaging.result: Age-appropriate volume loss. No structural lesions. No hydrocephalus. Midbrain normal size. No vascular changes.
imaging.reasoning: MRI is often NORMAL in PD but rules out important mimics: NPH (dilated ventricles), vascular parkinsonism (lacunar infarcts in basal ganglia), structural lesions, PSP (midbrain atrophy - 'hummingbird sign').

imaging.name: DaTscan (Dopamine Transporter SPECT)
imaging.result: Not ordered - clinical diagnosis clear. Would show reduced uptake in striatum.
imaging.reasoning: DaTscan shows dopamine transporter loss. MOST USEFUL when diagnosis uncertain: PD vs essential tremor, drug-induced parkinsonism, or psychogenic tremor. Abnormal in PD, normal in ET.

imaging.name: FDG-PET or Amyloid PET (research settings)
imaging.result: Not indicated clinically.
imaging.reasoning: Research tool. May help distinguish PD from atypical parkinsonism. Not routine clinical practice.

procedures.name: Levodopa Challenge Test
procedures.result: Levodopa trial will be initiated by neurology to confirm diagnosis and assess response.
procedures.reasoning: ROBUST response to levodopa supports PD diagnosis. Poor response suggests atypical parkinsonism (MSA, PSP). This is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

procedures.name: Formal Cognitive Testing (MoCA)
procedures.result: MoCA 26/30 - mild cognitive impairment. Deficits in visuospatial and executive function.
procedures.reasoning: Baseline cognitive assessment. Mild deficits common in PD. Early dementia would suggest DLB instead.

procedures.name: Physical Therapy Evaluation
procedures.result: PT evaluation ordered for gait training, balance exercises, and fall prevention.
procedures.reasoning: PT is CRITICAL in PD. LSVT-BIG program specifically designed for PD. Addresses gait, balance, posture, and functional mobility.

procedures.name: Occupational Therapy Evaluation
procedures.result: OT evaluation for ADL assessment and adaptive strategies.
procedures.reasoning: OT helps with fine motor tasks, dressing, eating. Home safety evaluation.

procedures.name: Speech Therapy Evaluation (if hypophonia/dysphagia)
procedures.result: Speech therapy referral for voice training (LSVT-LOUD program).
procedures.reasoning: LSVT-LOUD is evidence-based speech therapy for PD hypophonia. Also assess swallowing if dysphagia present.

treatments.name: Carbidopa-Levodopa (Sinemet)
treatments.result: Will be initiated by neurology. Typical starting dose: 25/100 TID. Gold standard treatment for PD motor symptoms.
treatments.reasoning: LEVODOPA remains gold standard for PD motor symptoms. Most effective medication. Initiated by neurology or movement disorders specialist.

treatments.name: Dopamine Agonist (pramipexole, ropinirole)
treatments.result: May be considered as initial therapy or adjunct. Discuss with neurology.
treatments.reasoning: Alternative initial therapy, especially in younger patients (delay levodopa motor complications). Side effects: impulse control disorders, sleepiness, hallucinations.

treatments.name: MAO-B Inhibitor (selegiline, rasagiline)
treatments.result: May be added for mild symptoms or as adjunct to levodopa.
treatments.reasoning: Mild symptomatic benefit. May be used as monotherapy for mild symptoms or added to levodopa to extend 'on' time.

treatments.name: Fall Prevention Counseling
treatments.result: Counseled on home safety: remove throw rugs, install grab bars, improve lighting, night lights. Referral for home safety evaluation.
treatments.reasoning: Falls are major cause of morbidity in PD. Proactive home modification reduces fall risk. PT for balance training.

treatments.name: Orthostatic Hypotension Management
treatments.result: Counseled on slow position changes, compression stockings, hydration, salt intake. Fludrocortisone or midodrine if symptomatic.
treatments.reasoning: Orthostatic hypotension present (20 mmHg drop). Contributes to falls. Conservative measures first; medications if needed.

treatments.name: Constipation Management
treatments.result: Counseled on high-fiber diet, hydration, exercise. Polyethylene glycol (MiraLAX) daily PRN.
treatments.reasoning: Constipation is common non-motor symptom. Can precede motor symptoms by years. Manage proactively.

treatments.name: Depression Screening and Treatment
treatments.result: PHQ-9 completed. Score 8 - mild depression. Will monitor and consider SSRI if worsens.
treatments.reasoning: Depression affects 40-50% of PD patients. Screen and treat. SSRIs generally safe in PD.

treatments.name: Driving Safety Counseling
treatments.result: Discussed driving safety concerns given bradykinesia and reaction time. Recommend driving evaluation if concerned.
treatments.reasoning: Driving safety must be addressed. Reaction time, motor control, and cognitive function all affect driving. Formal evaluation may be needed.

consults.name: Movement Disorders Neurology
consults.result: Referral to movement disorders specialist for confirmation, medication initiation, and long-term management.
consults.reasoning: Movement disorders specialist should confirm diagnosis and initiate treatment. Expertise in medication titration and managing motor complications.

consults.name: Physical Therapy
consults.result: PT referral for gait training, balance, and LSVT-BIG program.
consults.reasoning: PT is ESSENTIAL in PD - as important as medication. LSVT-BIG is PD-specific program with evidence for improving function.

consults.name: Social Work
consults.result: Social work consulted for resources, support groups, advance care planning discussion.
consults.reasoning: Connect with PD support groups. Discuss advance care planning early while patient can participate. Community resources.

disposition.name: Outpatient Management with Specialty Referral
disposition.result: Referred to movement disorders neurology. MRI and labs ordered. PT referral placed. Follow-up in 2-4 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: PD is chronic, outpatient condition. Specialist referral for medication initiation and long-term care plan.

disposition.name: Patient/Family Education
disposition.result: Educated patient and wife on PD, treatment expectations, and importance of exercise. Provided written materials and support group information.
disposition.reasoning: Education is crucial. Set realistic expectations - medications help but don't cure. Emphasize importance of PT/exercise.

disposition.name: Advance Care Planning Discussion
disposition.result: Introduced topic of advance directives while patient is cognitively intact. Will discuss further at follow-up.
disposition.reasoning: Early discussion of goals of care while patient can fully participate. PD is progressive - planning ahead is important.

actualDiagnosis.name: Parkinson's Disease
keyFindings: Bradykinesia on finger tapping and rapid alternating movements
Shuffling gait with reduced arm swing
Non-motor symptoms: REM sleep behavior disorder, constipation, hyposmia
teachingPoints: PD diagnosis requires BRADYKINESIA plus either rest tremor OR rigidity. Tremor alone is not sufficient.
REST tremor (decreases with action) distinguishes PD from essential tremor (increases with action)
ASYMMETRIC onset is characteristic of idiopathic PD. Symmetric parkinsonism suggests other causes.
Non-motor symptoms often precede motor symptoms: constipation, hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, depression
Always check medications - metoclopramide, antipsychotics, and others cause drug-induced parkinsonism
Orthostatic hypotension is common in PD - check it and manage it to prevent falls
Early cognitive decline or visual hallucinations suggest Lewy body dementia rather than PD

guidelineReferences.name: AAN Practice Parameter: Neuroprotective Strategies and Alternative Therapies for PD
keyPoints: Levodopa remains most effective treatment for motor symptoms
MAO-B inhibitors may be considered for early disease
Exercise and physical therapy should be recommended for all patients
No proven neuroprotective therapy exists
Coenzyme Q10, vitamin E not recommended for neuroprotection

guidelineReferences.name: MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria for Parkinson's Disease
guidelineReferences.citation: Postuma RB et al. Mov Disord. 2015;30(12):1591-1601
keyPoints: Core criterion: Parkinsonism = bradykinesia + rest tremor OR rigidity
Supportive criteria: clear response to dopaminergic therapy, rest tremor, olfactory loss, cardiac sympathetic denervation
Exclusionary criteria: cerebellar abnormalities, vertical gaze palsy, limb apraxia, cortical sensory loss
Red flags for atypical parkinsonism: rapid progression, early falls, early dementia, early dysautonomia
DaTscan useful when diagnosis uncertain (PD vs essential tremor, drug-induced)

closureScript.diagnosis: Based on my examination, you have Parkinson's disease. This is a condition where a part of your brain that controls movement isn't making enough dopamine. That's why you have the tremor, stiffness, and slowness.
closureScript.plan: I'm referring you to a movement disorder neurologist who specializes in this. There are very effective medications that can significantly improve your symptoms, especially the stiffness and slowness. Physical therapy is also important. We'll check some blood tests and get a brain MRI to make sure there's nothing else going on.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: We need to address fall prevention - your balance is affected. Consider removing throw rugs at home. If you have any sudden worsening, difficulty swallowing, or changes in your thinking, let us know. Also, some medications can make parkinsonism worse - always check before starting new medicines.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this diagnosis is difficult to hear. But Parkinson's is a slowly progressive disease, and many people live well with it for many years, especially with treatment. The medications can make a big difference in how you feel and function.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the tremor was worse with action?
whatIfScenarios.description: Tremor that worsens when reaching for objects, better at rest

chiefComplaint.original: My hand shakes, especially when I'm sitting still
chiefComplaint.modified: My hands shake when I try to use them - eating soup is impossible. It's fine when my hands are resting.

feedback.correct: Essential tremor = ACTION tremor (worse with use, better at rest). Bilateral (often asymmetric but both sides). Improves with alcohol. No bradykinesia/rigidity. Treatment: propranolol, primidone. Can coexist with PD.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of essential tremor. What features distinguish it from PD?
feedback.incorrect: Essential tremor vs Parkinson's: ET = action tremor (worse using hands), bilateral, often familial, improves with alcohol, NO bradykinesia/rigidity. PD = REST tremor (improves with use), asymmetric, bradykinesia required, doesn't improve with alcohol.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Rest tremor = PD. Action tremor = Essential tremor. The relationship between tremor and movement is the key distinction. ET is 10x more common than PD.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was on metoclopramide?
whatIfScenarios.description: Parkinsonism in patient taking metoclopramide for nausea

chiefComplaint.original: My hand shakes, especially when I'm sitting still
chiefComplaint.modified: My hand started shaking a few months ago. I've been taking Reglan for my stomach.

feedback.correct: Metoclopramide, antipsychotics, and other dopamine blockers cause parkinsonism. ALWAYS check medications. Stop offending drug - symptoms often improve over weeks to months. May unmask underlying early PD.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of drug-induced cause. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Drug-induced parkinsonism: metoclopramide (Reglan), antipsychotics, prochlorperazine (Compazine), valproic acid can all cause parkinsonism. Often SYMMETRIC (vs asymmetric idiopathic PD). Treatment: stop the drug. May take months to resolve. ALWAYS ask about medications.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Idiopathic PD = asymmetric onset, no medication cause. Drug-induced = medication history, often symmetric, reversible. Check medications in ALL patients with parkinsonism.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if symptoms were symmetric and rapidly progressive?
whatIfScenarios.description: Symmetric parkinsonism progressing over months, not years

chiefComplaint.original: My hand shakes, especially when I'm sitting still
chiefComplaint.modified: Both sides affected from the start, and I've gotten much worse in just 6 months

keyDiscriminators: Red flags (falls, autonomic failure, eye movement abnormalities)

feedback.correct: Red flags for atypical parkinsonism: symmetric onset, rapid progression, early falls, poor levodopa response, severe autonomic failure, vertical gaze palsy (PSP), cerebellar signs (MSA). Worse prognosis than idiopathic PD.
feedback.partial: Good concern for atypical parkinsonism. What are the red flags?
feedback.incorrect: Atypical parkinsonism red flags: symmetric onset, rapid progression, early falls (within 1 year), severe autonomic failure, vertical gaze palsy, cerebellar signs, poor levodopa response. Suggests PSP, MSA, CBD rather than idiopathic PD. Different prognosis and management.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Idiopathic PD = asymmetric, slow progression, good levodopa response. Atypical = symmetric, rapid, poor levodopa response, additional features. Recognition matters for prognosis and treatment planning.

keyPoints: Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism
REM sleep behavior disorder supportive
Avoid antipsychotics - severe sensitivity

male-74-dlb.name: George Price
male-74-dlb.chiefComplaint: He's seeing things that aren't there and getting confused

missedConsequences.Parkinson's Disease Dementia: Delayed recognition affects treatment approach and prognosis counseling.
missedConsequences.Delirium: Treatable delirium mistaken for dementia progression leads to missed reversible causes.

Misdiagnosing as Alzheimer's - DLB has different features and treatment implications
Missing the fluctuations - family says 'good days and bad days' which is characteristic
Not recognizing REM sleep behavior disorder as part of the syndrome

mcqs.question: Which feature distinguishes dementia with Lewy bodies from Parkinson's disease dementia?

options.explanation: Both can have visual hallucinations.

options.text: Timing - dementia occurs within 1 year of motor symptoms in DLB
options.explanation: The '1-year rule': if dementia develops within 1 year of parkinsonism → DLB. If parkinsonism precedes dementia by >1 year → PD with dementia.

options.text: Presence of REM sleep behavior disorder
options.explanation: RBD occurs in both conditions.
Both may respond to levodopa, though response may be more limited in DLB.

mcqs.question: Why are antipsychotics particularly dangerous in DLB?

options.explanation: Sedation is a concern but not the main danger.

options.text: Severe neuroleptic sensitivity causing rigidity, immobility, and potentially death
options.explanation: DLB patients have extreme sensitivity to dopamine-blocking drugs. Antipsychotics can cause severe parkinsonism, autonomic instability, and death.
Cognitive effects are a concern but not the primary danger.
This is not the primary concern.

allDiagnoses.name: Dementia with Lewy Bodies
allDiagnoses.rationale: Visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, parkinsonism, and REM sleep behavior disorder - classic DLB constellation.

allDiagnoses.name: Parkinson's Disease Dementia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect parkinsonism to precede dementia by >1 year. Here, cognitive symptoms are prominent early.

allDiagnoses.name: Delirium
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fluctuating cognition can look like delirium. Must rule out infection, metabolic causes, medications.

allDiagnoses.name: Alzheimer's Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: AD typically has prominent memory loss without visual hallucinations or parkinsonism early.

allDiagnoses.name: Vascular Dementia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect stepwise decline and vascular risk factors/imaging findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
allDiagnoses.rationale: Treatable cause of dementia with gait problems. Need to rule out with imaging.

male-74-dlb.oldcartsNarrative: (Wife reporting): "Something's very wrong with my husband. For the past several months, he's been seeing people who aren't there - usually children or animals in the house. He'll have conversations with them. Some days he's almost normal - sharp, knows what's going on. Other days he's completely confused, stares into space, barely recognizes me. He moves slowly now, shuffles when he walks, and has a tremor. At night, he acts out his dreams - punching, kicking, yelling. He fell out of bed twice. Last week his doctor gave him Haldol for the hallucinations and he became stiff as a board - couldn't move. We had to stop it."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about what he sees.
historyQuestions.answer: Children playing in the living room. A cat that walks through. Sometimes people standing in the corner. He describes them very clearly, with details. He knows they probably aren't real but sees them anyway.
historyQuestions.reasoning: VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS, well-formed and detailed, are core feature of DLB. Often people or animals.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned good days and bad days - describe that.
historyQuestions.answer: It's dramatic. Monday he was doing crossword puzzles, chatting normally. Tuesday he couldn't figure out how to use the TV remote, stared blankly, barely spoke. It changes hour to hour sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: FLUCTUATING COGNITION with pronounced variation in attention and alertness is core feature of DLB.

historyQuestions.text: When did the movement problems start?
historyQuestions.answer: The slowness and tremor started maybe 6 months ago, around the same time as the confusion. Maybe the confusion was a little before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cognitive and motor symptoms within 1 year of each other = DLB by convention. Motor first by >1 year = PD dementia.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the nighttime behaviors.
historyQuestions.answer: He's always been a quiet sleeper. Now he thrashes, punches, kicks, yells out. Acting out dreams, he says - fighting or running. He's hurt me and himself. Fell out of bed.
historyQuestions.reasoning: REM SLEEP BEHAVIOR DISORDER - acting out dreams - is supportive feature of DLB and often precedes other symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: What happened with the Haldol?
historyQuestions.answer: Within 2 days he could barely move. Stiff, couldn't walk, could barely swallow. We stopped it and he slowly got better, but it scared us badly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NEUROLEPTIC SENSITIVITY - severe reaction to antipsychotics - is highly characteristic of DLB. Can be fatal.

historyQuestions.text: Any fainting, constipation, or urinary problems?
historyQuestions.answer: He's had constipation for years. Gets dizzy when he stands up. Had a couple of near-fainting episodes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, constipation) is supportive feature of DLB.

historyQuestions.text: Is it mainly memory problems or more confusion and attention?
historyQuestions.answer: He can remember things from the past pretty well. It's more that he zones out, can't focus, gets confused about what's happening right now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Attention and executive dysfunction more prominent than memory loss distinguishes DLB from AD.

historyQuestions.text: Has he seemed depressed?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, he's been down. Less interested in things. Sleeps a lot during the day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Depression is supportive feature of DLB.

historyQuestions.text: What medications does he take?
historyQuestions.answer: Blood pressure medicine - lisinopril. Aspirin. They tried Haldol for the hallucinations but that was terrible. Nothing else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical - must avoid antipsychotics and anticholinergics in DLB

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies, but after what happened with Haldol I'm terrified to give him anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important; neuroleptic sensitivity documented

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Had a TIA a few years ago but recovered fully. Otherwise healthy until all this started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: TIA history relevant - vascular component possible

historyQuestions.text: What was he like before all this started?
historyQuestions.answer: Sharp as a tack. Retired engineer. Did the taxes, managed finances, read constantly. This change has been devastating.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishes high baseline function - makes decline more apparent

historyQuestions.text: Any recent fevers, coughs, urinary symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: No fever. No cough. He's had some urinary accidents lately but no burning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rule out UTI or other infection causing delirium superimposed on dementia

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

housing_safety.answer: We have stairs. I found him trying to go down at 3 AM once, confused. We put up a baby gate.
housing_safety.relevance: Fall risk from parkinsonism, fluctuating consciousness, and nocturnal confusion requires home safety modifications.

social_caregiver_status.answer: I'm his only caregiver. Our kids help when they can but they work. It's exhausting, especially the nights.
social_caregiver_status.relevance: Caregiver burden is significant in DLB. Nighttime behaviors are particularly taxing. Respite care needed.

social_sleep_arrangements.answer: We sleep in separate beds now because of his thrashing. I have to check on him constantly.
social_sleep_arrangements.relevance: RBD management - bed rails, floor padding, separate beds may be needed for safety.

housing_driving.answer: I took his keys away after he got lost driving to the grocery store he's been to for 30 years.
housing_driving.relevance: Driving cessation appropriate given cognitive fluctuations and impaired visuospatial function.

social_medications_management.answer: I give him all his pills now. He can't keep track anymore.
social_medications_management.relevance: Medication management by caregiver essential. Must avoid antipsychotics, anticholinergics.

social_future_planning.answer: We haven't talked about what happens when I can't take care of him anymore. It's hard to think about.
social_future_planning.relevance: Advance care planning important given progressive nature of DLB. Earlier conversations are better.

physicalExam.finding: BP 138/82 sitting, 105/65 standing (33 mmHg drop!). Symptomatic - feels lightheaded on standing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Marked orthostatic hypotension - autonomic dysfunction supportive of DLB. Major fall risk.

physicalExam.finding: Alert but attention fluctuates during exam - sometimes engaged, sometimes stares vacantly. Oriented to person and place, not date. Difficulty with complex commands. Visual attention poor. MoCA 18/30 with deficits in attention, executive function, visuospatial.
physicalExam.reasoning: Fluctuating attention visible during exam. Visuospatial and attention deficits characteristic of DLB.

physicalExam.finding: Mild bilateral rest tremor. Moderate cogwheel rigidity bilaterally. Bradykinesia - slow finger tapping with decrement. Shuffling gait, reduced arm swing, mild postural instability.
physicalExam.reasoning: Parkinsonism present but relatively SYMMETRIC (unlike PD which is asymmetric). Core feature of DLB.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly male with masked facies, stooped posture. Quiet voice. Occasionally appears to be looking at something not visible to examiner.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypomimia and hypophonia from parkinsonism. Visual hallucination behavior observed.

physicalExam.finding: When asked, patient reports seeing a small child sitting in the corner of the room. Describes clothing in detail. Acknowledges child 'probably isn't real' but sees it clearly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Well-formed visual hallucinations with retained insight are classic for DLB.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Rule out infection contributing to confusion. Anemia can worsen cognition.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Rule out metabolic causes of confusion - hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, uremia.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Hypothyroidism is reversible cause of cognitive slowing. Always check.

labs.name: Vitamin B12
labs.reasoning: B12 deficiency causes reversible dementia and can worsen DLB symptoms.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.reasoning: Rule out UTI as cause of fluctuating confusion. Common cause of delirium in elderly.

labs.name: RPR
labs.reasoning: Neurosyphilis is rare but treatable cause of dementia. Part of standard workup.

labs.name: HIV (if risk factors)
labs.reasoning: Consider in patients with risk factors. HIV dementia is reversible with treatment.

imaging.name: MRI Brain
imaging.result: Mild generalized atrophy. Relative preservation of medial temporal lobes/hippocampi (unlike AD). No significant vascular disease. No hydronephrosis. No masses or NPH.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out structural causes (tumor, NPH, stroke). Hippocampal preservation (vs. AD atrophy) supports DLB. Absence of significant vascular disease argues against vascular dementia.

imaging.name: DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT)
imaging.result: Reduced dopamine transporter uptake in basal ganglia bilaterally - INDICATIVE biomarker for DLB.
imaging.reasoning: DaTscan shows dopaminergic deficit in DLB (and PD). Helps confirm diagnosis when uncertain. Normal DaTscan argues against DLB.

imaging.name: FDG-PET (if diagnosis uncertain)
imaging.result: Not routinely needed with classic presentation.
imaging.reasoning: Shows occipital hypometabolism in DLB (cingulate island sign). Useful when diagnosis unclear.

treatments.name: Donepezil (Aricept) - FIRST-LINE
treatments.result: Donepezil 5mg daily started. Will increase to 10mg after 4-6 weeks if tolerated. Cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line for DLB.
treatments.reasoning: Cholinesterase inhibitors are FIRST-LINE for DLB cognitive symptoms AND may reduce visual hallucinations. DLB patients often respond better than AD patients. Start low, increase slowly.

treatments.name: Rivastigmine (Exelon) - alternative
treatments.result: Alternative cholinesterase inhibitor. Available as patch (less GI side effects).
treatments.reasoning: Rivastigmine patch may be better tolerated than oral medications. Good alternative if donepezil not tolerated.

treatments.name: Memantine (add-on for moderate-severe)
treatments.result: Consider adding memantine for moderate-severe dementia.
treatments.reasoning: NMDA antagonist can be added to cholinesterase inhibitor in moderate-severe dementia. May help with agitation.

treatments.name: ⚠️ AVOID ANTIPSYCHOTICS - CRITICAL
treatments.result: CRITICAL: NO typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, etc.). Severe neuroleptic sensitivity can cause rigidity, sedation, falls, and DEATH. If absolutely necessary: quetiapine or pimavanserin only, at lowest doses.
treatments.reasoning: NEUROLEPTIC SENSITIVITY is a core feature of DLB. Antipsychotics cause severe worsening in 50%+ of DLB patients. This patient already had severe reaction to Haldol. Document prominently in chart.

treatments.name: ⚠️ AVOID ANTICHOLINERGICS
treatments.result: Avoid medications with anticholinergic effects (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, TCAs, etc.). Will worsen cognition.
treatments.reasoning: Anticholinergics directly counteract cholinesterase inhibitors and worsen cognition. Review all medications for anticholinergic burden.

treatments.name: Orthostatic hypotension management
treatments.result: Counseled on slow position changes, compression stockings, adequate hydration. Consider midodrine or fludrocortisone if severe.
treatments.reasoning: Orthostatic hypotension is major fall risk. Non-pharmacologic measures first. Midodrine 2.5-10mg TID or fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily if needed.

treatments.name: Melatonin for REM sleep behavior disorder
treatments.result: Melatonin 3-6mg at bedtime for RBD. Safe first-line option.
treatments.reasoning: RBD management: Melatonin is first-line (safe). Clonazepam 0.25-0.5mg is alternative but increases fall risk. Safety measures: bed rails, mattress on floor, remove bedside objects.

treatments.name: Carbidopa-levodopa for parkinsonism (cautious)
treatments.result: May consider low-dose carbidopa-levodopa if motor symptoms significantly impair function. Use cautiously - can worsen hallucinations.
treatments.reasoning: Levodopa can help motor symptoms but may worsen hallucinations and confusion. Start very low, increase slowly. Often less effective than in PD.

treatments.name: SSRI for depression (if present)
treatments.result: If depression significant, can use SSRI (sertraline, citalopram). Avoid TCAs (anticholinergic).
treatments.reasoning: Depression common in DLB. SSRIs are safe. Avoid TCAs (anticholinergic), SNRIs may worsen orthostatic hypotension.

consults.name: Neurology/Movement Disorders
consults.result: Referred to neurologist specializing in movement disorders/dementia for ongoing management.
consults.reasoning: Specialist management recommended for DLB. Complex medication decisions require expertise.

consults.name: Geriatric Psychiatry (if severe behavioral symptoms)
consults.result: Available if behavioral symptoms require specialized management.
consults.reasoning: Geriatric psychiatrist can help with hallucinations, agitation if not controlled with cholinesterase inhibitors.

consults.name: Palliative Care (for goals of care)
consults.result: Consider early palliative care involvement for goals of care discussions.
consults.reasoning: DLB is progressive and has worse prognosis than AD. Early goals of care discussions are important.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with close follow-up
disposition.result: Home with family. Neurology follow-up in 4-6 weeks. Return sooner if worsening.
disposition.reasoning: Most DLB patients managed outpatient with caregiver support. Close follow-up needed for medication titration.

disposition.name: Caregiver education and support
disposition.result: Provided education materials on DLB. Referral to support group. Social work consult for respite care options.
disposition.reasoning: Caregiver burden is enormous in DLB. Support groups, respite care, and education critical for sustainable home care.

disposition.name: Home safety planning
disposition.result: Occupational therapy home safety evaluation ordered. Discussed: remove throw rugs, install grab bars, bed rails, nightlights, secure stairs.
disposition.reasoning: Fall risk is very high (parkinsonism + orthostatic hypotension + fluctuating attention). Home safety modifications essential.

disposition.name: Medication alert card
disposition.result: Provided card listing DLB diagnosis and medications to AVOID (antipsychotics, anticholinergics). Patient should carry at all times.
disposition.reasoning: In emergency settings, patient may receive contraindicated medications. Alert card is literally life-saving.

disposition.name: Driving cessation counseling
disposition.result: Confirmed driving has stopped. Discussed alternative transportation options.
disposition.reasoning: Driving is unsafe with fluctuating attention, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism. Family already stopped him.

disposition.name: Advance care planning
disposition.result: Recommended completing advance directives and healthcare proxy while patient has capacity to participate.
disposition.reasoning: DLB is progressive. Early discussion while patient can participate in decisions. Document wishes regarding hospitalization, feeding tubes, resuscitation.

actualDiagnosis.name: Dementia with Lewy Bodies
keyFindings: Fluctuating cognition with marked variations in attention
Severe neuroleptic sensitivity (reacted badly to Haldol)
Relative preservation of memory compared to attention/visuospatial
Cognitive symptoms within 1 year of motor symptoms
teachingPoints: Core features of DLB: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behavior disorder
1-year rule: Dementia within 1 year of parkinsonism = DLB; parkinsonism >1 year before dementia = PD dementia
NEVER give typical antipsychotics to DLB patients - neuroleptic sensitivity can cause severe morbidity and death
If antipsychotic absolutely necessary, only use quetiapine or pimavanserin at lowest doses with extreme caution
Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine) are first-line treatment and may help hallucinations
Visual hallucinations in DLB are often well-formed (people, animals) and patient may have insight
Autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, constipation) common - manage to prevent falls
Caregiver support is critical - this is a very difficult disease to manage at home

guidelineReferences.name: DLB Consortium Criteria for Clinical Diagnosis
keyPoints: Core features: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, REM sleep behavior disorder, parkinsonism
Probable DLB: dementia + ≥2 core features OR 1 core + ≥1 indicative biomarker
Indicative biomarkers: reduced DaT uptake, abnormal MIBG cardiac scintigraphy, REM sleep without atonia on PSG
Supportive features: severe neuroleptic sensitivity, autonomic dysfunction, hypersomnia, hyposmia
The 1-year rule: dementia before or within 1 year of parkinsonism = DLB

guidelineReferences.name: AAN Practice Parameter: Management of Dementia
keyPoints: Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine) first-line treatment for cognitive and behavioral symptoms
AVOID typical antipsychotics - extreme neuroleptic sensitivity in DLB
If antipsychotic absolutely necessary: quetiapine or pimavanserin at lowest effective dose
Carbidopa-levodopa for parkinsonism but may worsen hallucinations
Melatonin or clonazepam for REM sleep behavior disorder

closureScript.diagnosis: Your husband has dementia with Lewy bodies - a condition where abnormal protein deposits affect brain areas controlling thinking, movement, and behavior. This explains the hallucinations, the dramatic day-to-day changes, the movement problems, and the dream-acting behavior.
closureScript.plan: I'm referring him to a neurologist who specializes in this condition. We'll start a medication called a cholinesterase inhibitor that can help with the thinking and may reduce the hallucinations. We need to manage his blood pressure drops to prevent falls. Most importantly, certain medications - especially antipsychotics - are very dangerous for him, as you saw with the Haldol.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If any doctor wants to prescribe an antipsychotic for the hallucinations, please tell them about his diagnosis and what happened before. This is critical. Also, his fall risk is high - we need to make the home safer. And please call if there's sudden worsening - we should make sure there's no infection or other treatable cause.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is an overwhelming diagnosis. It's a progressive condition, but there are treatments that can help with symptoms. We'll also connect you with support resources - caregiver support groups, respite care options, and social work. You can't do this alone, and you shouldn't have to.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if memory loss was the primary symptom without hallucinations?
whatIfScenarios.description: Progressive memory loss over 2 years, no hallucinations, no movement problems

chiefComplaint.original: He's seeing people who aren't there
chiefComplaint.modified: He keeps asking the same questions. He can't remember what happened yesterday but remembers 30 years ago.

mustNotMiss: Reversible causes of dementia (B12, thyroid, NPH)

feedback.correct: Memory-predominant dementia without hallucinations or parkinsonism = Alzheimer's disease most likely. Still rule out reversibles (B12, TSH, RPR). MRI to rule out vascular/structural. Cholinesterase inhibitors are treatment.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of Alzheimer's pattern. What distinguishes it from DLB?
feedback.incorrect: Alzheimer's disease: memory loss is early and predominant, gradual progression, no early hallucinations or parkinsonism. DLB: hallucinations, fluctuations, parkinsonism, RBD. The symptom profile determines the diagnosis. Treatment similar (cholinesterase inhibitors) but antipsychotic safety differs.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: AD = memory predominant, gradual, no hallucinations early. DLB = hallucinations, fluctuations, parkinsonism, RBD. Both respond to cholinesterase inhibitors but only DLB has neuroleptic sensitivity.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if parkinsonism preceded cognitive decline by 5 years?
whatIfScenarios.description: Known Parkinson's for 5 years, now developing hallucinations and cognitive decline

chiefComplaint.original: He's seeing people who aren't there
chiefComplaint.modified: He's had Parkinson's for 5 years. Now he's seeing things that aren't there and getting confused.

feedback.correct: Motor symptoms >1 year before dementia = PD dementia (by convention). Same pathology as DLB. First rule out medication side effects (dopamine agonists cause hallucinations) and delirium. Treatment similar to DLB - rivastigmine has best evidence.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of PD dementia. What's the practical difference from DLB?
feedback.incorrect: 1-year rule: Parkinsonism >1 year before dementia = PD dementia. Dementia within 1 year of parkinsonism = DLB. Same underlying pathology (Lewy bodies), different clinical labels. Both need caution with antipsychotics. Check dopaminergic medications as cause of hallucinations.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: DLB = dementia at onset or within 1 year of parkinsonism. PD dementia = well-established PD, then dementia develops later. Same pathology, different timing. Both have neuroleptic sensitivity.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the confusion was acute with recent infection?
whatIfScenarios.description: Known mild cognitive impairment, now acutely confused with UTI

chiefComplaint.original: He's seeing people who aren't there
chiefComplaint.modified: He's had mild memory problems, but over 2 days he's suddenly much more confused, agitated, and seeing things. He's had urinary symptoms.

feedback.correct: ACUTE confusion = DELIRIUM, not new dementia. Find the cause - UTI is common in elderly. Treat the underlying cause, delirium resolves. Dementia patients are MORE vulnerable to delirium. Don't attribute acute changes to 'just dementia.'
feedback.partial: Good recognition of delirium. What's the approach?
feedback.incorrect: Delirium superimposed on dementia: acute change + precipitant (infection, medications, metabolic) = delirium. Must treat underlying cause. Dementia is a RISK FACTOR for delirium. Never attribute acute confusion to 'progression of dementia' without ruling out reversible causes.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Dementia = chronic, progressive over months-years. Delirium = acute, fluctuating, caused by medical illness/medication. Always look for reversible cause when dementia patient has acute worsening.

guidelineReferences.name: ATSDR Mercury Toxicological Profile
keyPoints: Neurological effects predominant
Different toxicity by form (elemental, organic, inorganic)
Chelation with DMSA or DMPS

female-35-mercury.name: Sarah Clark
female-35-mercury.chiefComplaint: My hands are numb and shaky, and I've been having trouble concentrating
female-35-mercury.variantDescription: Chronic methylmercury toxicity from fish consumption

missedConsequences.Mercury Poisoning: Continued exposure leads to permanent neurological damage. Especially critical if patient is pregnant (severe fetal effects).

commonPitfalls: Not asking detailed dietary history including fish consumption
Attributing symptoms to anxiety or 'stress'
Not checking mercury level
Missing pregnancy (teratogenic effects)
Not identifying the specific fish causing exposure

allDiagnoses.name: Mercury Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: High fish consumption + peripheral neuropathy + tremor + cognitive symptoms + elevated mercury level = mercury toxicity.

allDiagnoses.name: Essential Tremor
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not explain sensory symptoms or cognitive changes.

allDiagnoses.name: Multiple Sclerosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sensory symptoms and cognitive changes warrant consideration, but fish history points to mercury.

allDiagnoses.name: Peripheral Neuropathy (other)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Need to identify cause - mercury is the cause here.

allDiagnoses.name: Anxiety Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not explain objective tremor and sensory loss.

female-35-mercury.oldcartsNarrative: "For the past few months, my hands and feet have been numb and tingly. I've also developed a tremor in my hands - I can't write as well as I used to. I've been having trouble concentrating at work and I feel like I'm in a fog. My vision has been a bit blurry too. I've always been healthy - I eat really well, lots of fish because it's healthy. I'm actually trying to get pregnant so I've been eating even more fish for the omega-3s."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about how much fish you eat
historyQuestions.answer: A lot! Fish for lunch and dinner most days. I love swordfish and tuna steaks. Trying to be healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: HIGH consumption of large predatory fish (swordfish, tuna) = high mercury intake

historyQuestions.text: What types of fish specifically?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly swordfish - it's my favorite. Also tuna steaks, shark when I can get it. Maybe 10-12 servings per week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Swordfish, shark, king mackerel, tilefish, tuna have HIGHEST mercury. 10-12 servings/week is massive exposure.

historyQuestions.text: How long have you been eating this much fish?
historyQuestions.answer: Increased it about a year ago when I started trying to get pregnant. Heard fish was good for fertility.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Ironically increased mercury exposure while trying to conceive - mercury is teratogenic

historyQuestions.text: Describe the numbness
historyQuestions.answer: Started in my fingertips, now up to my wrists. Same with toes up to ankles. Constant tingling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Glove-stocking sensory neuropathy is classic for mercury toxicity

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the tremor
historyQuestions.answer: My hands shake when I try to do things - writing, using chopsticks. Worse when I'm tired.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Intention tremor is classic neurological sign of mercury toxicity

historyQuestions.text: Describe the concentration problems
historyQuestions.answer: Brain fog. I'm making mistakes at work I never used to make. Hard to find words sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cognitive impairment including word-finding difficulty is neurotoxic effect

historyQuestions.text: Are you currently pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. We've been trying for 6 months without success. About to see a fertility specialist.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Mercury causes infertility AND severe fetal damage. Must rule out pregnancy.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just prenatal vitamins. That's it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prenatal vitamins - tragically ironic given mercury consumption

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important if chelation therapy considered

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been healthy. This is all so strange.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Previously healthy - symptoms are from mercury exposure

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 weeks ago. Pretty regular.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timing important for pregnancy status and counseling

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

water_food_raw_foods.answer: I eat fish almost every day. Swordfish is my favorite. Also lots of tuna steaks.
water_food_raw_foods.relevance: Swordfish has among highest mercury content of any fish. FDA recommends AVOIDING it entirely for women who might become pregnant.

water_food_ethnic_foods.answer: I love sushi. Probably have it 3-4 times a week.
water_food_ethnic_foods.relevance: Additional mercury exposure from sushi - bigeye tuna sushi is particularly high in mercury.

hobbies_hunting_fishing_rec.answer: My husband fishes and we eat what he catches - bass from the local lake.
hobbies_hunting_fishing_rec.relevance: Locally caught fish from contaminated waters can have high mercury depending on location.

occupation_current_job_title.answer: I work in an office. No chemical exposure that I know of.
occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Rules out occupational mercury exposure - this is dietary.

chemicals_medications_supplements.answer: Just prenatal vitamins since we're trying to conceive.
chemicals_medications_supplements.relevance: No other mercury sources. Ironically taking prenatals while consuming teratogenic amounts of mercury.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in glove-stocking distribution to wrists/ankles
physicalExam.reasoning: Peripheral sensory neuropathy - classic mercury finding

physicalExam.finding: Intention tremor on finger-nose testing. Fine motor coordination impaired.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cerebellar signs (intention tremor) are characteristic of mercury toxicity

physicalExam.finding: Constriction of peripheral visual fields bilaterally
physicalExam.reasoning: Visual field constriction ('tunnel vision') is classic mercury neurotoxicity

physicalExam.finding: Mildly ataxic gait. Difficulty with tandem walking.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cerebellar ataxia from mercury neurotoxicity

physicalExam.finding: Mild difficulty with word finding. Serial 7s slowed. Recent memory slightly impaired.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cognitive impairment is well-documented effect of mercury

labs.name: Whole blood mercury
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: Markedly elevated blood mercury confirms toxicity. Blood mercury reflects recent exposure (half-life ~70 days for methylmercury).

labs.name: 24-hour urine mercury
labs.reasoning: Urine mercury better reflects inorganic mercury; blood is better for organic/methylmercury. Both elevated confirms significant total body burden.

labs.name: Hair mercury
labs.result: Would be elevated - can confirm chronic exposure pattern
labs.reasoning: Hair mercury shows exposure over time (1cm = ~1 month). Useful for documenting chronic exposure.

labs.name: Pregnancy test
labs.result: Negative - CRITICAL to rule out pregnancy
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Must rule out pregnancy. Methylmercury is severely teratogenic (Minamata disease). Patient actively trying to conceive - this is urgent.

labs.name: CBC and CMP
labs.result: CBC normal. CMP normal including renal function.
labs.reasoning: Baseline labs. Mercury can cause renal toxicity (more with inorganic forms).

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Mercury can affect thyroid function. Rule out concurrent thyroid disease.

imaging.name: MRI Brain
imaging.result: Subtle bilateral cerebellar atrophy. T2 hyperintensity in occipital cortex bilaterally. No other focal lesions.
imaging.reasoning: MRI can show cerebellar atrophy and occipital cortex changes in severe methylmercury toxicity. Documents CNS involvement.

procedures.name: EMG/Nerve conduction studies
procedures.result: Sensory > motor axonal polyneuropathy. Reduced sensory amplitudes in distal nerves.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms peripheral neuropathy pattern. Provides baseline for monitoring recovery.

procedures.name: Formal visual field testing
procedures.result: Bilateral concentric constriction of visual fields ('tunnel vision').
procedures.reasoning: Formal documentation of visual field loss. Classic pattern for mercury toxicity.

treatments.name: STOP ALL HIGH-MERCURY FISH (CRITICAL)
treatments.result: IMMEDIATE cessation of swordfish, shark, king mackerel, tilefish, bigeye tuna. This is THE treatment.
treatments.reasoning: Removal of exposure is primary treatment. Methylmercury elimination half-life is ~70 days - levels will slowly decline once exposure stops.

treatments.name: Chelation NOT routinely recommended
treatments.result: Chelation (DMSA, DMPS) has limited efficacy for organic/methylmercury. Not recommended unless extremely severe.
treatments.reasoning: Unlike lead or inorganic mercury, chelation has minimal role in methylmercury. May even redistribute mercury. Main treatment is stopping exposure.

treatments.name: STRICT contraception until levels normalize
treatments.result: MUST use reliable contraception. DO NOT conceive until blood mercury <10 mcg/L.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Methylmercury is severely teratogenic. Causes Minamata disease - cerebral palsy, developmental delay, microcephaly. Must prevent pregnancy until levels normal.

treatments.name: Selenium supplementation (emerging)
treatments.result: Consider selenium 200mcg daily - may help with mercury detoxification.
treatments.reasoning: Selenium binds mercury and may enhance elimination. Some evidence for benefit but not standard of care.

treatments.name: Supportive care for symptoms
treatments.result: Gabapentin 100mg TID for neuropathic symptoms. Physical therapy for balance/coordination.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic treatment for neuropathy. PT for ataxia and coordination. Symptoms may slowly improve over months to years.

consults.name: Medical Toxicology
consults.result: Confirms dietary methylmercury toxicity from fish consumption. Chelation not indicated. Removal of exposure is key. Serial monitoring recommended.
consults.reasoning: Toxicology expertise for confirmation, treatment guidance, and monitoring plan.

consults.name: Neurology
consults.result: Will follow for neuropathy and cerebellar symptoms. Baseline EMG/NCS obtained. Monitor for improvement.
consults.reasoning: Neurological monitoring for recovery. Some improvement expected but neurological damage may be partially permanent.

consults.name: OB/GYN or Reproductive Medicine
consults.result: Referral for counseling about fertility and mercury. Must delay conception attempts.
consults.reasoning: Critical counseling about pregnancy timing. Patient was trying to conceive - this changes everything.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Stable for outpatient management. Close follow-up arranged.
disposition.reasoning: Chronic mercury toxicity managed outpatient. No acute life-threatening issues.

disposition.name: Serial mercury level monitoring
disposition.result: Repeat blood mercury in 1 month, then every 2-3 months until normal. Expected decline with half-life ~70 days.
disposition.reasoning: Monitor for declining levels confirming adequate exposure removal. Levels should halve approximately every 2.5 months.

disposition.name: Detailed fish consumption counseling
disposition.result: AVOID: Swordfish, shark, king mackerel, tilefish, bigeye tuna. SAFE (in moderation): Salmon, sardines, anchovies, herring, shrimp, tilapia, pollock. Limit to 12oz/week.
disposition.reasoning: Specific dietary guidance essential. Patient can still eat fish - just low-mercury varieties. FDA 12oz/week limit for women who may become pregnant.

disposition.name: Pregnancy timing counseling
disposition.result: DO NOT attempt pregnancy until blood mercury <10 mcg/L. May take 6-12+ months. Will coordinate with OB/GYN.
disposition.reasoning: This is often the hardest news for patients. Clear timeline helps with expectations. Mercury must clear before conception is safe.

disposition.name: Prognosis counseling

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a condition called manganism - it's caused by breathing in manganese fumes from welding over many years. It causes Parkinson's-like symptoms, which is why it's sometimes confused with Parkinson's disease. But it affects a different part of the brain, which is why the Parkinson's medication didn't work for you.
closureScript.plan: The most important thing is that you cannot continue welding - ever. Continued exposure will make this worse. We can try some medications to help with your symptoms, but I have to be honest that they won't work as well as they do for regular Parkinson's. I'm referring you to a movement disorders specialist and to occupational medicine.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you go back to welding, your condition will get worse. Even with stopping exposure, some symptoms may remain permanent, though many patients stabilize or improve somewhat. The mood symptoms may improve more than the movement symptoms.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is devastating news about your career. You should qualify for workers' compensation and potentially disability. This is a recognized occupational disease. The occupational medicine team will help document everything and guide you through the process.

male-68-weakness.teachingPoint: This patient needs tPA NOW. Time is brain - every 15 minutes of delay to treatment reduces good outcomes by ~4%. Your job on neurology: recognize stroke (FAST - Face, Arms, Speech, Time), get CT STAT, identify last known well time, calculate NIHSS, and alert the stroke team. This patient also has large vessel occlusion on CTA - he should go to thrombectomy after tPA. Don't wait for any labs except glucose before giving tPA in a patient not on anticoagulation.
male-68-weakness.clinicalPearl: The 'door-to-needle' time (arrival to tPA administration) should be <60 minutes. Know the major tPA contraindications: hemorrhage on CT, BP >185/110 that won't come down, platelets <100K, INR >1.7, recent major surgery, prior intracranial hemorrhage, recent stroke, symptoms improving rapidly (TIA). Also remember: a patient found with symptoms at wake-up is NOT automatically excluded - the last known well is when they went to sleep, and advanced imaging can identify tPA candidates.

historyQuestions.reasoning: True vertigo (spinning) from vestibular dysfunction.

historyQuestions.text: Can you walk?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I tried and I kept falling to my left. I can't even stand without holding onto something.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Severe gait ataxia out of proportion to vertigo = cerebellar sign.

historyQuestions.text: Any weakness in your arms or legs?
historyQuestions.answer: No weakness - I can move everything fine, I just can't balance.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cerebellar stroke can present with ataxia WITHOUT weakness.

historyQuestions.text: Any double vision?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't think so.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diplopia would suggest brainstem involvement.

historyQuestions.text: Any headache?
historyQuestions.answer: No headache.

historyQuestions.text: What medical problems do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure, high cholesterol. I take medications for both.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vascular risk factors increase stroke probability.

physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated BP common in acute stroke.

physicalExam.finding: Appears ill, holding onto bed rail. Eyes closed. Vomited once during exam.
physicalExam.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting common in both central and peripheral vertigo.

physicalExam.finding: Direction-changing nystagmus - beats LEFT when looking left, beats RIGHT when looking right. Does NOT suppress with fixation.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Direction-changing nystagmus = CENTRAL. Peripheral nystagmus beats in ONE direction.

physicalExam.finding: HI (Head Impulse): NORMAL bilaterally - no corrective saccade. N (Nystagmus): Direction-changing, doesn't suppress. TS (Test of Skew): Positive - vertical misalignment on cover-uncover test.
physicalExam.reasoning: CENTRAL HINTS: Normal head impulse + direction-changing nystagmus + skew deviation = STROKE until proven otherwise.

physicalExam.finding: Finger-to-nose: Overshoots on left (left-sided dysmetria). Heel-to-shin: Abnormal on left. Rapid alternating movements: Dysdiadochokinesia on left.
physicalExam.reasoning: Left cerebellar signs - cerebellum controls ipsilateral limbs.

physicalExam.finding: Unable to walk unassisted. With support, wide-based gait, veers/falls to LEFT.
physicalExam.reasoning: Falls toward side of cerebellar lesion.

physicalExam.finding: 5/5 strength in all extremities. No drift.

female-58-vertigo.teachingPoint: This is a deadly miss if you don't know HINTS. On your neurology rotation, MEMORIZE: central HINTS = normal head impulse test, direction-changing nystagmus, and skew deviation. The key teaching point: in peripheral vertigo, the head impulse test is ABNORMAL (you see a catch-up saccade when you turn the head toward the affected ear). A NORMAL head impulse test in someone with acute vertigo means the vestibular nerve is working - so the problem must be central (stroke). Also remember: CT is BAD at seeing posterior fossa - don't be falsely reassured by a negative CT.
female-58-vertigo.clinicalPearl: The saying goes: 'You can't walk? You can't go home.' Patients with peripheral vertigo (like vestibular neuritis) are miserable but can usually walk - unsteadily but without falling. A patient who cannot walk AT ALL or keeps falling to one side has a cerebellar stroke until proven otherwise. Also know that cerebellar strokes can swell and cause brainstem compression/herniation - these patients need ICU monitoring and neurosurgery on standby.

male-42-first.oldcartsNarrative: [Wife witnessed]: "We were watching TV and suddenly he made a strange sound, went stiff, then started shaking all over. His eyes rolled back. He was shaking for maybe a minute - it felt like forever. He bit his tongue - there's blood. He wet himself. After it stopped, he was out of it for about 20 minutes - couldn't answer questions, confused. He's more alert now but has a bad headache. He's never had anything like this. He's been healthy. Maybe drinking a bit more lately - we've had stress. He has a headache most mornings now that I think about it. No drugs."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me exactly what you saw.
historyQuestions.answer: He made this grunting sound, his whole body went stiff with arms out, then he started jerking - arms, legs, everything. His eyes rolled back. It lasted about a minute. Then he just went limp.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic generalized tonic-clonic: initial tonic phase (stiffening) → clonic phase (rhythmic jerking) → post-ictal.

historyQuestions.text: Did he bite his tongue?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, there's blood on his lips. Let me look - yes, it's cut on the side.
historyQuestions.reasoning: LATERAL tongue bite is very specific for seizure. Tip of tongue bite can be syncope.

historyQuestions.text: Did he lose bladder or bowel control?
historyQuestions.answer: He wet himself during the shaking.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Urinary incontinence common in generalized seizures, rare in syncope.

historyQuestions.text: How was he after the shaking stopped?
historyQuestions.answer: He was totally out of it for probably 20 minutes. Didn't know where he was, couldn't answer questions. Gradually came back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Prolonged post-ictal confusion (>5 min) strongly suggests seizure vs syncope (rapid recovery).

historyQuestions.text: Has this ever happened before? Any staring spells, jerking, or strange feelings?
historyQuestions.answer: Never anything like this. No staring spells. Wait - he has mentioned smelling burnt toast sometimes for no reason over the past few months.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IMPORTANT: Olfactory hallucinations can be auras from temporal lobe seizures - may indicate structural lesion.

historyQuestions.text: Any headaches recently?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, he's had morning headaches for the past month or so. We thought it was stress.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CONCERNING: Morning headaches + new seizure in adult = must rule out brain tumor or increased ICP.

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol does he drink?
historyQuestions.answer: He's been drinking more lately, maybe 4-5 beers most nights. But he had some last night - he's not in withdrawal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol withdrawal seizures occur 6-48h after last drink. This patient drank last night - less likely withdrawal.

historyQuestions.text: Any medications or drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: No medications. No recreational drugs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Drug use (cocaine, stimulants) can cause seizures.

physicalExam.finding: BP 152/94 (elevated), HR 98, Temp 98.6°F. Glucose: 108.
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated BP and HR expected post-ictally. Glucose rules out hypoglycemia.

physicalExam.finding: Awake, alert now. Slightly confused when asked detailed questions. Knows his name and where he is but not the exact date. Appears fatigued.
physicalExam.reasoning: Resolving post-ictal state - improving but still mildly confused.

physicalExam.finding: No head trauma. Lateral tongue laceration on left side with blood. Oral mucosa otherwise intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lateral tongue bite - highly specific for seizure.

physicalExam.finding: Cranial nerves intact. Motor: 5/5 throughout, no drift. Sensation intact. Reflexes 2+ symmetric. Gait steady. No papilledema on fundoscopy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal neuro exam between seizures is typical, but focal findings would suggest structural lesion.

physicalExam.finding: No papilledema. Sharp disc margins bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Papilledema would suggest increased ICP - concerning for mass.

imaging.result: Hypodense lesion right temporal lobe, approximately 2cm, with surrounding edema. No hemorrhage. Mild mass effect.
imaging.reasoning: MANDATORY in first seizure. This CT is concerning - needs MRI.

imaging.name: MRI Brain with and without contrast
imaging.result: 2.2cm ring-enhancing lesion right temporal lobe. Surrounding vasogenic edema. Findings concerning for primary brain neoplasm vs metastasis.
imaging.reasoning: Better characterization of lesion. This patient has a brain tumor.

labs.reasoning: Baseline.
