male-42-first.teachingPoint: This case illustrates why EVERY first seizure needs brain imaging. The morning headaches and olfactory hallucinations ('burnt toast') were subtle clues of a temporal lobe lesion. New-onset seizure in an adult over 40 has a significant chance of being caused by a structural lesion. Treatment priorities: 1) Control acute seizure if ongoing, 2) Image the brain, 3) Treat underlying cause (this patient needs neurosurgery referral), 4) Consider AED for seizure prophylaxis while awaiting definitive treatment.
male-42-first.clinicalPearl: Know the differences between seizure and syncope: Seizure = lateral tongue bite, prolonged confusion (>5 min), tonic-clonic activity >30 seconds, cyanosis during event, post-ictal headache/fatigue. Syncope = brief (<15 sec) jerking if any, rapid recovery (<1 min confusion), prodrome of lightheadedness/warmth, pallor during event. Also remember: the 'aura' is actually a focal seizure - olfactory hallucinations localize to temporal lobe.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the seizure had been ongoing for 10 minutes?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient is still seizing when EMS arrives - continuous GTC activity

chiefComplaint.original: He had a seizure at work
chiefComplaint.modified: EMS: He's been seizing for 10 minutes, we gave midazolam but he's still going

feedback.correct: STATUS EPILEPTICUS = seizure >5 min or recurrent without return to baseline. EMERGENCY. Protocol: 1) Benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4mg IV or midazolam IM), 2) If continues 5+ min: second-line AED (fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, or valproate), 3) If still seizing: intubation + propofol/midazolam drip + EEG.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of status epilepticus. What's the treatment escalation?
feedback.incorrect: Status epilepticus is life-threatening - mortality 20%. Treatment ladder: 1) Benzodiazepines, 2) Second-line AED (fosphenytoin 20mg/kg, levetiracetam 60mg/kg, valproate 40mg/kg), 3) Refractory SE: intubation + continuous infusion (propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital) with continuous EEG.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Brief self-limited seizure = supportive care, workup. Status epilepticus = medical emergency, aggressive treatment escalation. Duration determines urgency.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had stopped drinking 48 hours ago?
whatIfScenarios.description: Heavy drinker who stopped cold turkey 2 days ago, now seizing

chiefComplaint.original: He had a seizure at work
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been drinking a fifth of vodka daily for years. I stopped 2 days ago because I felt sick. Then I had a seizure.

keyDiscriminators: Still need imaging if first seizure

feedback.correct: Alcohol withdrawal seizures peak 24-48 hours after last drink. But 30% progress to DTs if untreated. Treatment: benzodiazepines (prevent more seizures AND DTs), thiamine BEFORE glucose, still image brain (alcoholics have high rate of subdural hematomas). Admit for monitoring.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of withdrawal seizure. What complication must you prevent?
feedback.incorrect: Alcohol withdrawal seizures: typically 24-48h after cessation, often multiple (rum fits). Treatment: benzodiazepines (chlordiazepoxide, lorazepam) - treats seizures AND prevents DTs. Give thiamine before glucose. Still image brain - alcoholics fall and get subdurals. 30% risk of DTs without treatment.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: First seizure with structural cause = treat underlying lesion. Alcohol withdrawal seizure = benzodiazepines, monitor for DTs, still rule out structural cause. Context changes management but imaging still needed.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was 36 weeks pregnant?
whatIfScenarios.description: Pregnant woman with new seizure, found to have hypertension and proteinuria

chiefComplaint.original: He had a seizure at work
chiefComplaint.modified: 36 weeks pregnant, had a seizure. BP 180/110, 3+ protein on urine dip. Headache and visual changes beforehand.

shouldDrop: Epilepsy (new diagnosis in pregnancy is eclampsia until proven otherwise)

feedback.correct: ECLAMPSIA = seizure in preeclamptic patient. MAGNESIUM SULFATE is the treatment (not standard AEDs). 4-6g IV loading dose, then 1-2g/hr infusion. Control BP (labetalol, hydralazine). Delivery is the cure - urgent OB consult. Monitor for HELLP, DIC.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of eclampsia. What's the specific treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Eclampsia: seizure + preeclampsia (HTN + proteinuria). Treatment: MAGNESIUM SULFATE (better than phenytoin - MAGPIE trial). BP control with labetalol or hydralazine (avoid ACEi). Delivery is definitive treatment regardless of gestational age. Monitor: mag level, reflexes, respiratory status, HELLP labs.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Non-pregnant seizure = standard AEDs. Eclampsia = magnesium sulfate is the treatment. Pregnancy completely changes the approach.

mcqs.question: The patient mentions an episode of transient vision loss in one eye 3 weeks ago. What is this called and what does it suggest?

options.text: Amaurosis fugax - suggests retinal/ophthalmic artery ischemia from carotid disease
options.explanation: Amaurosis fugax is transient monocular vision loss from retinal ischemia, classically from carotid artery stenosis with emboli to the ophthalmic artery.

options.text: Homonymous hemianopia - suggests occipital lobe stroke
options.explanation: Homonymous hemianopia affects the same visual field in both eyes and indicates posterior circulation involvement.
Optic neuritis causes painful vision loss and is associated with MS, not TIA.
Migraine aura usually includes positive visual phenomena (sparkles) and is bilateral.

mcqs.question: You hear a right carotid bruit on exam. What is the sensitivity of carotid bruit for significant stenosis?

options.explanation: Carotid bruit actually has poor sensitivity (20-30%) for detecting stenosis.
Carotid bruit is less sensitive than this.

options.text: 20-30% - low sensitivity, patients can have stenosis without bruit
options.explanation: Carotid bruit has only 20-30% sensitivity. Many patients with significant stenosis have no bruit, and some bruits are transmitted cardiac murmurs.
It's not this uncommon - about 20-30% of patients with significant stenosis will have an audible bruit.

mcqs.question: This patient's ABCD2 score is 6-7. What is her approximate 2-day stroke risk?

options.explanation: That's the risk for score 0-3.
That's the risk for score 4-5.
ABCD2 score 6-7 carries approximately 8% 2-day stroke risk. These patients should be admitted for expedited workup.
The 7-day risk is about 12%, but 2-day risk is approximately 8%.

female-65-carotid.oldcartsNarrative: "About an hour ago, my speech suddenly got all slurred. My daughter said I sounded drunk but I hadn't been drinking. My left arm also felt weak - I dropped my fork and couldn't pick it up. It scared us both. But by the time we got here, maybe 45 minutes later, I was feeling much better. I can talk normally now and my arm feels fine. I almost didn't come in since it went away, but my daughter insisted. Oh, and a few weeks ago I had this strange thing where I suddenly couldn't see out of my right eye - like a curtain coming down. It went away after about an hour so I didn't think much of it."

historyQuestions.text: Exactly how long did the symptoms last?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe 45 minutes total. Started to get better in the car on the way here.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Duration is a key component of ABCD2 score. >60 min = 2 points, 10-59 min = 1 point.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the vision loss episode. Which eye? How long?
historyQuestions.answer: It was my right eye - like a shade being pulled down. Lasted maybe an hour then cleared up completely. I figured I was just tired.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Amaurosis fugax (monocular vision loss) strongly suggests ipsilateral carotid stenosis - this is the second TIA!

historyQuestions.text: Did you have any headache during or after the episode?
historyQuestions.answer: No headache at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: TIA/stroke typically don't cause headache. Headache with focal deficits raises concern for hemorrhage or migraine.

historyQuestions.text: Did anyone witness any shaking, jerking, or loss of consciousness?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. I was awake the whole time, just couldn't talk right.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out witnessed seizure activity. Todd's paralysis is a consideration but no seizure was witnessed.

historyQuestions.text: Any palpitations, chest pain, or shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my heart felt fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Palpitations might suggest AFib as embolic source. Still need ECG regardless.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, but I don't always take my medication. My doctor said my A1c was high last visit.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes is a component of ABCD2 score (+1 point) and major vascular risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Metformin for diabetes but I miss doses. Lisinopril for blood pressure. Some cholesterol pill.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Identifies poorly controlled risk factors - she's not on antiplatelet therapy.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, about a pack a day for 40 years. I know I should quit.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Major vascular risk factor contributing to carotid disease.

historyQuestions.text: When exactly did the slurred speech start?
historyQuestions.answer: About an hour ago. I was just sitting at the kitchen table having lunch and suddenly the words weren't coming out right.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Time of onset critical for potential thrombolytic therapy; sudden onset typical for vascular event

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, no allergies. I've taken different medicines over the years without any problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before antiplatelet therapy, contrast for imaging, and potential interventions

historyQuestions.text: Besides diabetes, what other medical problems do you have?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure, high cholesterol. I had a hysterectomy years ago. No heart problems that I know of.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple vascular risk factors; confirms no prior stroke, cardiac history

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries on your neck or blood vessels?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just the hysterectomy and my gallbladder out. Nothing on my neck or heart.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior carotid surgery would be relevant; no prior interventions

historyQuestions.text: Any stroke or heart disease in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother had a stroke in her 70s. My father died of a heart attack. Heart problems seem to run in the family.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of cerebrovascular and cardiovascular disease increases risk

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: A glass of wine with dinner occasionally. Maybe once or twice a week. Nothing heavy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Moderate alcohol intake - not a significant contributor to risk

historyQuestions.text: Who do you live with?
historyQuestions.answer: I live with my husband. My daughter was just visiting today, thank goodness she was there when it happened.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Support system important for monitoring and medication compliance after discharge

historyQuestions.text: Before this happened, how were you doing? Any memory problems or weakness?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been fine. Sharp as a tack, no memory issues. Walk every day, do my own shopping. This came out of nowhere.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Good baseline function makes this acute event more alarming; no prior deficits

historyQuestions.text: When did you last see your doctor?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 months ago. She told me my blood sugar and blood pressure were too high and wanted me to do better with my medicines.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Poorly controlled risk factors despite recent medical contact

historyQuestions.text: What are you most worried about?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm scared it's a stroke like my mother had. She never recovered fully. I don't want to end up in a nursing home or be a burden on my family.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Understanding fears helps with counseling about TIA significance and stroke prevention

actualDiagnosis.name: TIA with High-Grade Carotid Stenosis (Actually minor stroke on MRI)
teachingPoints: TIA IS A STROKE WARNING: 8% of high-risk TIA patients have stroke within 2 days!
ABCD2 SCORE: Age, BP, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes. Score ≥4 needs urgent workup.
AMAUROSIS FUGAX: Transient monocular vision loss = retinal TIA = carotid disease until proven otherwise
CAROTID BRUIT: Only 20-30% sensitive for stenosis. Absence doesn't rule it out!
MRI CHANGES DEFINITION: DWI+ lesion upgrades 'TIA' to 'minor stroke' - same urgent management
NASCET TRIAL: CEA reduces stroke risk from 26% to 9% at 2 years for symptomatic 70-99% stenosis
ANTIPLATELET THERAPY: Start immediately. ASA, Clopidogrel, or ASA+Dipyridamole all acceptable.
STATIN FOR ALL: Even with normal LDL - SPARCL trial showed 18% RRR for recurrent stroke

female-65-carotid.teachingPoint: This patient has a high-risk TIA (ABCD2 score 6) with symptomatic carotid stenosis. The prior amaurosis fugax was her first warning! With 75-80% stenosis, she's a candidate for carotid endarterectomy within 2-4 weeks per NASCET trial (reduces 2-year stroke risk from 26% to 9%). Key management: admit, antiplatelet therapy, statin, expedited carotid imaging, vascular surgery consult. Never dismiss 'resolved' neurological symptoms - TIA is a stroke warning sign!

feedback.correct: Crescendo TIA = multiple TIAs in 24-72 hours = VERY HIGH stroke risk. Treat like unstable angina of the brain. ICU admission, dual antiplatelet, heparin controversial but considered. Emergent vessel imaging - may need urgent CEA or stenting within 24-48 hours rather than 2 weeks.

If significant stenosis, CEA or CAS within 24-48 hours rather than standard 2-week window.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Single TIA = urgent workup within 24-48 hours, CEA within 2 weeks. Crescendo TIA = stroke emergency, intervention within 24-48 hours. Frequency determines urgency.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if cardiac monitoring showed atrial fibrillation?
whatIfScenarios.description: Extended monitoring reveals paroxysmal AFib

chiefComplaint.original: My left arm went weak and I couldn't speak clearly
chiefComplaint.modified: Same TIA presentation - but 7-day Holter shows 4-hour episode of atrial fibrillation

feedback.correct: AFib + TIA = cardioembolic etiology. Anticoagulation (DOACs preferred) superior to antiplatelet for AFib. Start after small infarct (usually wait 1-3 days for minor stroke, longer for larger). CHA2DS2-VASc guides long-term risk. This patient now needs anticoagulation, not just antiplatelet.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of AFib as cause. What's the treatment change?
feedback.incorrect: AFib-related TIA: anticoagulation is treatment (not antiplatelet alone). DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) preferred over warfarin. Start 1-3 days after minor stroke (longer if large infarct - rule of 1-3-6-12 days). CHA2DS2-VASc score guides risk but this patient already had event - needs anticoagulation.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: TIA from carotid stenosis = antiplatelet + CEA. TIA from AFib = anticoagulation. Source determines secondary prevention. Some patients have both.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if symptoms were vertigo, diplopia, and ataxia?
whatIfScenarios.description: Posterior circulation TIA symptoms

chiefComplaint.original: My left arm went weak and I couldn't speak clearly
chiefComplaint.modified: Sudden severe vertigo, double vision, couldn't walk straight - lasted 20 minutes then resolved. Never had this before.

feedback.correct: Posterior circulation TIA: vertigo, diplopia, ataxia, dysarthria, bilateral symptoms. Image posterior vessels (CTA includes vertebrals and basilar). Basilar stenosis or occlusion is devastating. Same urgency as anterior TIA. Consider vertebral dissection if neck pain or trauma.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of posterior symptoms. What vessel imaging is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Posterior circulation TIA: 'D's - Diplopia, Dysarthria, Dysphagia, Dizziness, Drop attacks. Plus ataxia, bilateral symptoms. CTA must include vertebrobasilar system. Basilar stroke is catastrophic - treat TIA urgently. Consider vertebral dissection (especially if young or neck pain). Same antiplatelet/statin approach.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Anterior TIA = face/arm/leg weakness, speech, vision in one eye. Posterior TIA = vertigo, diplopia, ataxia, bilateral symptoms. Territory determines which vessels to image.

male-68-afib-stroke.name: William Foster
male-68-afib-stroke.chiefComplaint: Sudden right-sided weakness and trouble speaking
male-68-afib-stroke.variantDescription: Acute ischemic stroke in AFib patient with subtherapeutic INR

missedConsequences.Acute Ischemic Stroke: Failure to administer tPA within window results in massive completed stroke with permanent dense hemiplegia, aphasia, and nursing home placement.
missedConsequences.Large Vessel Occlusion: Without CTA, LVO is missed. Patient could have benefited from mechanical thrombectomy up to 24h with good collaterals.
missedConsequences.Hemorrhagic Stroke: If tPA given without CT, hemorrhagic transformation causes death. CT is essential before thrombolytics.

commonPitfalls: Waiting for all labs before CT - only glucose and coags needed before tPA decision
Treating blood pressure too aggressively - only treat if >185/110 for tPA, >220/120 otherwise
Missing the history of dietary vitamin K increase reducing warfarin efficacy
Not recognizing the prior 'clumsiness' as a TIA warning sign
Forgetting to calculate door-to-needle time - target is <60 minutes

mcqs.question: The patient's daughter mentions he's been eating more vegetables lately. Why is this clinically significant?

options.explanation: While fiber is healthy, this is not the acute concern here.

options.text: Vitamin K in vegetables antagonizes warfarin
options.explanation: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Increased dietary vitamin K reduces warfarin's anticoagulant effect, raising stroke risk in AFib patients.

options.text: Vegetables can cause drug interactions with metoprolol
options.explanation: No significant interaction between vegetables and metoprolol.

options.text: High potassium from vegetables affects cardiac rhythm
options.explanation: While relevant for some conditions, this is not the key issue here.

mcqs.question: The patient's NIHSS is 18. Which of the following is the most appropriate interpretation?

options.text: Too severe to benefit from tPA
options.explanation: Even severe strokes (NIHSS >25) may benefit from reperfusion therapy. NIHSS 18 is moderate-severe and absolutely warrants treatment.

options.text: Moderate-severe stroke, tPA candidate, also consider thrombectomy
options.explanation: NIHSS 16-20 is moderate-severe. Patient is a tPA candidate if within window and no contraindications. Given severity, should also evaluate for large vessel occlusion and thrombectomy.

options.text: Minor stroke, tPA unlikely to provide benefit
options.explanation: NIHSS 1-4 is minor. NIHSS 18 is NOT minor.

options.text: Score indicates hemorrhagic rather than ischemic stroke
options.explanation: NIHSS assesses stroke severity regardless of type. CT is needed to rule out hemorrhage.

mcqs.question: The CT head shows no hemorrhage and the INR is 1.4. Which statement about tPA eligibility is correct?

options.text: INR 1.4 is too high - tPA contraindicated
options.explanation: tPA is contraindicated if INR >1.7. INR 1.4 is acceptable.

options.text: Must wait for INR to normalize before tPA
options.explanation: INR 1.4 is below the 1.7 threshold. No need to wait.

options.text: INR <1.7 is acceptable for tPA if within window
options.explanation: tPA is contraindicated if INR >1.7. This patient's INR of 1.4 does not exclude tPA. Interestingly, his subtherapeutic INR likely contributed to his stroke.

options.text: tPA dose should be reduced for any anticoagulation
options.explanation: tPA dose is not adjusted based on INR. It's either given at full dose (0.9mg/kg) or not at all.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Ischemic Stroke (MCA)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden onset focal neuro deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) + AFib + subtherapeutic INR = classic cardioembolic stroke.

allDiagnoses.name: TIA
allDiagnoses.rationale: Symptoms persist >60 min and NIHSS 18. By definition, this is now a stroke, not TIA.

allDiagnoses.name: Hemorrhagic Stroke
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must rule out with CT BEFORE any thrombolytics. Can present similarly.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypoglycemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause focal neurological deficits that mimic stroke. Check glucose immediately.

allDiagnoses.name: Seizure (postictal paralysis)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Todd's paralysis can mimic stroke, but no seizure witnessed.

allDiagnoses.name: Migraine with aura
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hemiplegic migraine possible but no headache and wrong age/presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Brain tumor
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually more gradual onset. CT would show mass.

allDiagnoses.name: Subdural hematoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with focal deficits, especially in anticoagulated patients. CT required.

male-68-afib-stroke.oldcartsNarrative: "He was fine one minute - we were having lunch together - and then suddenly he slumped over in his chair. He tried to talk to me but his words were all jumbled and slurred. His right arm just dropped and he couldn't lift it. That was about 45 minutes ago. He didn't pass out or shake. He's been taking warfarin for his irregular heartbeat. He's been really good about eating healthy lately - lots of salads and vegetables. Actually, yesterday he was clumsier than usual and dropped his coffee mug, which was strange for him."

historyFindings.medicationHistory: Warfarin 5mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily, metoprolol 50mg BID. Reports taking all medications consistently.
historyFindings.socialHistory: Never smoker. Occasional glass of wine. Retired teacher. Lives with wife, daughter nearby.

examFindings.general: Alert but frustrated. Attempting to speak with significant effort. Moderate distress.
examFindings.heent: Right gaze deviation. Pupils 3mm equal and reactive. No facial droop at rest, but RIGHT facial weakness with attempted smile (lower face).
examFindings.cardiac: IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR rhythm (atrial fibrillation). No murmurs.
examFindings.neuro: NIHSS 18. LOC: Alert (0). LOC Questions: Gets one wrong (1). LOC Commands: Does one (1). Gaze: RIGHT gaze preference, partial (1). Visual: RIGHT homonymous hemianopia (1). Facial: RIGHT lower face weakness (2). Motor Arm: RIGHT 1/5 (4), Left 5/5 (0). Motor Leg: RIGHT 2/5 (3), Left 5/5 (0). Ataxia: Cannot assess (0). Sensory: RIGHT decreased (1). Language: BROCA'S APHASIA - non-fluent, effortful, comprehension relatively intact (2). Dysarthria: Severe (2). Extinction: Present (1).

labFindings.bmp: Na 140, K 4.2, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 18, Cr 1.0, Glucose 142

imagingFindings.ctHead: NO acute intracranial hemorrhage. No large hypodense lesion (too early). No midline shift. HYPERDENSE LEFT MCA SIGN - suggests acute thrombus. Small vessel ischemic changes.
imagingFindings.ecg: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular rate at 88 bpm. No acute ST changes.
imagingFindings.ctaHeadNeck: LEFT MCA M1 OCCLUSION. Good collateral flow. Carotid arteries patent.

immediateActions: Blood glucose checked at bedside (142 mg/dL)
keyDecisions: BP 178/102 - below 185/110 threshold, no need to treat before tPA
INR 1.4 - below 1.7 threshold, tPA not contraindicated
Symptom onset 48 minutes ago - well within 4.5h window
NIHSS 18 with M1 occlusion - thrombectomy candidate
clinicalCourse.treatment: tPA 0.9mg/kg (max 90mg) administered. 10% as bolus, 90% as infusion over 60 min. CTA showed M1 occlusion. Patient taken for mechanical thrombectomy with successful recanalization.

keyLessons: TIME IS BRAIN: Every minute of ischemia kills ~1.9 million neurons. Door-to-needle target <60 min.
DIETARY VITAMIN K: Increased intake antagonizes warfarin. Ask about dietary changes in anticoagulated patients.
TIA WARNING: His prior day's clumsiness was likely a TIA. 15% of strokes are preceded by TIA.
SUBTHERAPEUTIC INR: AFib patients with INR <2 have same stroke risk as no anticoagulation.
tPA ELIGIBILITY: INR must be <1.7, not normalized. Platelets >100K. Glucose 50-400 mg/dL.
HYPERDENSE MCA SIGN: Acute thrombus appears bright on non-contrast CT. Specific but not sensitive.
THROMBECTOMY: For large vessel occlusions (ICA, M1, M2, basilar) - can be done up to 24h with good imaging selection.

male-68-afib-stroke.teachingPoint: This patient had a cardioembolic stroke from his atrial fibrillation. His INR was subtherapeutic (1.4 instead of 2-3) likely due to increased dietary vitamin K from vegetables. The prior day's clumsiness was probably a TIA. Key management: (1) Immediate CT to rule out hemorrhage; (2) Check glucose and INR; (3) Calculate NIHSS (his was 18); (4) If within window and no contraindications, give tPA 0.9mg/kg; (5) Get CTA to evaluate for large vessel occlusion; (6) If LVO, proceed to thrombectomy. His INR of 1.4 was BELOW the 1.7 threshold so tPA was not contraindicated. Post-stroke: optimize anticoagulation, likely switch to DOAC.

guidelineReferences.name: ASCO Spinal Cord Compression Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Defined standard of care for malignant cord compression
keyPoints: EMERGENCY - time to treatment determines neurologic outcome
High-dose dexamethasone immediately if suspected
MRI of entire spine within hours
Surgery if single-level compression, good prognosis, neurologically deteriorating
Radiation therapy for radiosensitive tumors or poor surgical candidates
Maintain bowel/bladder function as priority

male-65-lung-mets.name: Henry Smith
male-65-lung-mets.chiefComplaint: Worsening back pain with new leg weakness
male-65-lung-mets.variantDescription: Metastatic lung cancer with malignant spinal cord compression

missedConsequences.Malignant Spinal Cord Compression: Progressive paralysis becomes irreversible. Patient who was walking becomes permanently paraplegic with loss of bladder/bowel function.
missedConsequences.Epidural Abscess: Untreated infection leads to sepsis and permanent neurological deficit.
missedConsequences.Cauda Equina Syndrome: Delay in diagnosis results in permanent incontinence and sexual dysfunction.

commonPitfalls: Attributing back pain to 'degenerative disease' without considering red flags
Not asking about urinary symptoms - retention is a late but critical finding
Ordering lumbar MRI when symptoms localize to thoracic spine
Waiting for oncology consult before starting dexamethasone
Not ordering WHOLE-spine MRI - 30% have multiple levels of disease

mcqs.question: Which feature of this patient's back pain is most concerning for spinal cord compression?

options.explanation: Chronic duration alone is not specific. Many benign conditions cause chronic back pain.

options.text: Pain worse when lying flat and with coughing/straining
options.explanation: Pain worse with recumbency and Valsalva maneuvers is classic for spinal cord compression. Degenerative pain is usually better lying down.
Location alone is nonspecific. The quality and associated features matter more.
Pain intensity alone doesn't distinguish compression from other causes.

mcqs.question: The patient has 3/5 strength bilaterally with bilateral Babinski signs. What does this pattern indicate?

options.explanation: LMN lesions cause hyporeflexia and downgoing toes. Babinski is an UMN sign.

options.text: Upper motor neuron lesion above the conus medullaris
options.explanation: Bilateral Babinski signs and hyperreflexia indicate upper motor neuron dysfunction - the lesion is in the spinal cord above where the UMN synapses with LMN (above L1-L2).
Cauda equina affects nerve roots (LMN) - would cause hyporeflexia and normal/absent Babinski.
Babinski is an involuntary reflex and cannot be 'faked.'

mcqs.question: MRI shows epidural compression at T8 with cord edema. What is the most important prognostic factor?

options.explanation: Mass size affects treatment planning but not prognosis as much as function.
Ambulatory status before treatment is the strongest predictor of outcome. Patients who are ambulatory at diagnosis: 80% remain so. Paraparetic: 50% walk again. Paraplegic: <10% recover.
While histology affects overall prognosis, neurological outcome depends more on baseline function.
Multiple levels affect treatment planning but baseline function still determines recovery.

allDiagnoses.name: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression
allDiagnoses.rationale: Progressive back pain + weight loss + smoking history + leg weakness + bladder dysfunction = classic presentation. Lung cancer is #1 cause.

allDiagnoses.name: Epidural Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present similarly. Usually has fever and elevated WBC. Surgical emergency.

allDiagnoses.name: Epidural Hematoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider if on anticoagulation or post-procedure. Usually more acute.

allDiagnoses.name: Cauda Equina Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: If lesion at L1-S4, causes LMN pattern. Saddle anesthesia, retention, hyporeflexia.

allDiagnoses.name: Degenerative Disc Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could cause pain but not weight loss or progressive neuro deficits.

allDiagnoses.name: Vertebral Compression Fracture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could be pathologic fracture from mets. Would show on imaging.

allDiagnoses.name: Transverse Myelitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Inflammatory cord disease, usually younger patients, can cause similar neuro findings but no bony lesions.

male-65-lung-mets.oldcartsNarrative: "This back pain started about 4 months ago. It was just a dull ache at first, maybe a 3 out of 10, but now it's a 6 and getting worse. It feels like a band around my chest and stomach area. The strange thing is it's actually worse when I lie down at night - I've been sleeping in a recliner. Coughing or straining makes it shoot through me. I've lost about 10 pounds without trying - my pants are loose. I haven't seen a doctor in years. Today the nurses are worried because I couldn't urinate last night and then this morning I wet myself on the way to the bathroom. My legs feel weaker too, and they're numb. I used to smoke a lot - about a pack a day for 40 years, but I quit 5 years ago."

historyFindings.socialHistory: Quit smoking 5 years ago, 40 pack-years prior. Occasional beer. Retired construction worker. Lives with wife.
historyFindings.familyHistory: Father died of lung cancer at 68.

examFindings.lungs: DECREASED BREATH SOUNDS RIGHT BASE. No wheezes or crackles.
examFindings.back: POINT TENDERNESS over T8-T10 vertebrae with palpation.
examFindings.neuro: Upper extremities: Strength 5/5, reflexes 2+, sensation intact. Lower extremities: Strength 3/5 bilateral (hip flexors weaker than extensors), reflexes 3+ (brisk/hyperreflexic), BILATERAL BABINSKI PRESENT, sensation decreased to pinprick and light touch from umbilicus (T10) down, vibration sense decreased. Rectal: DECREASED SPHINCTER TONE.

labFindings.cbc: WBC 8.2, Hgb 10.8 (anemia of chronic disease), Plt 285
labFindings.bmp: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 101, CO2 25, BUN 16, Cr 1.2, Glucose 98
labFindings.lft: AST 42, ALT 38, Alk Phos 312 (elevated - bone mets?)

imagingFindings.cxr: 4 cm RIGHT HILAR MASS with associated right lower lobe atelectasis. Multiple RIB LESIONS suspicious for metastases.
imagingFindings.mriSpine: T8 vertebral body collapse with EPIDURAL EXTENSION causing >50% SPINAL CANAL NARROWING. Spinal cord compression with CORD EDEMA. Additional lesions at T5, T11, L2 - multiple levels involved.

keyDecisions: Dexamethasone started BEFORE MRI confirmation - time critical
WHOLE spine MRI ordered (not just symptomatic level)
Radiation oncology vs. neurosurgery for decompression
Tissue diagnosis needed - bronchoscopy vs CT-guided biopsy
clinicalCourse.treatment: Continued dexamethasone 4mg q6h. Radiation oncology initiated emergency XRT to T8 level. Bronchoscopy confirmed non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma). Staging completed. Started on systemic therapy after radiation.

keyLessons: RED FLAGS FOR BACK PAIN: Weight loss, age >50 with new back pain, cancer history, progressive neuro deficits, bladder dysfunction, pain worse lying down.
CLASSIC PROGRESSION: Pain → Motor weakness → Sensory loss → Autonomic (bladder/bowel) - by the time bladder involved, often irreversible.
AMBULATORY STATUS IS PROGNOSIS: 80% who are walking stay walking. <10% who are paraplegic ever walk again.
START STEROIDS EARLY: Dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus, then 4-6mg q6h. Reduces vasogenic edema. Don't wait for MRI.
WHOLE-SPINE MRI: 30% have multiple levels. Imaging only symptomatic level misses additional disease.
COMMON PRIMARY TUMORS: Lung (17%), Breast (19%), Prostate (24%). 8-34% present with cord compression as FIRST sign of cancer.
PAIN CHARACTER: Worse lying flat and with Valsalva = structural spinal pathology. Degenerative pain is usually better lying down.

male-65-lung-mets.teachingPoint: This patient has malignant spinal cord compression from metastatic lung cancer - a medical emergency. Key features: (1) Progressive back pain worse lying flat and with Valsalva; (2) Constitutional symptoms (weight loss); (3) Smoking history; (4) Neurological progression from pain to weakness to bladder dysfunction. Management: (1) Give dexamethasone immediately (10mg IV); (2) Order WHOLE-spine MRI; (3) Urgent oncology and radiation oncology consults; (4) Consider surgery vs radiation for decompression. Prognosis depends on ambulatory status at diagnosis - this patient still has 3/5 strength so has a chance of maintaining ambulation if treated urgently.
